questions.question_text: Why is it not legal to transmit a 3 kHz bandwidth USB signal with a carrier frequency of 14.348 MHz?
choices: USB is not used on 20-meter phone
The lower 1 kHz of the signal is outside the 20-meter band
The upper 1 kHz of the signal is outside the 20-meter band

llm.prose_fact: It is not legal to transmit a 3 kHz bandwidth USB signal with a carrier frequency of 14.348 MHz on 20 meters because the upper 1 kHz of the signal extends beyond the band edge at 14.350 MHz.
llm.answer_explanation: USB voice signals extend from the carrier frequency upward by their bandwidth, so a 3 kHz USB signal at 14.348 MHz occupies 14.348 to 14.351 MHz. This causes the top 1 kHz of the signal to fall outside the authorized 20-meter band, making the transmission illegal.
llm.last_candidate: It is not legal to transmit a 3 kHz bandwidth USB signal with a carrier frequency of 14.348 MHz on 20 meters because the upper 1 kHz of the signal extends beyond the band edge at 14.350 MHz.

questions.question_text: When using a transceiver that displays the carrier frequency of phone signals, which of the following displayed frequencies represents the lowest frequency at which a properly adjusted LSB emission will be totally within the band?
choices: 300 Hz above the lower band edge
1 kHz above the lower band edge
3 kHz above the lower band edge

llm.prose_fact: When using a transceiver that displays the carrier frequency of phone signals, the lowest frequency at which a properly adjusted LSB emission will be totally within the band is 3 kHz above the lower band edge.
llm.answer_explanation: An LSB phone signal occupies bandwidth extending above the displayed carrier frequency, so the carrier must be set high enough to keep the entire sideband inside the band. A 3 kHz offset above the lower band edge ensures that a typical 3 kHz-wide LSB signal remains completely within the authorized frequency range.
llm.last_candidate: When using a transceiver that displays the carrier frequency of phone signals, the lowest frequency at which a properly adjusted LSB emission will be totally within the band is 3 kHz above the lower band edge.

questions.question_text: What is the highest legal carrier frequency on the 20-meter band for transmitting a 2.8 kHz wide USB data signal?

llm.prose_fact: The highest legal carrier frequency on the 20-meter band for transmitting a 2.8 kHz wide USB data signal is 14.1472 MHz.
llm.answer_explanation: The upper band edge for 20 meters is 14.150 MHz, and FCC rules require that the entire transmitted signal, including its 2.8 kHz bandwidth, remain within the band. Subtracting 2.8 kHz from 14.150 MHz results in a maximum carrier frequency of 14.1472 MHz.
llm.last_candidate: The highest legal carrier frequency on the 20-meter band for transmitting a 2.8 kHz wide USB data signal is 14.1472 MHz.

questions.question_text: May an Extra class operator answer the CQ of a station on 3.601 MHz LSB phone?
choices: Yes, the entire signal will be inside the SSB allocation for Extra class operators
Yes, the displayed frequency is within the 75-meter phone band segment
No, the sideband components will extend beyond the edge of the phone band segment
No, US stations are not permitted to use phone emissions below 3.610 MHz

llm.prose_fact: An Extra class operator may not answer the CQ of a station on 3.601 MHz LSB phone because the sideband components will extend beyond the edge of the 75-meter phone band segment.
llm.answer_explanation: In LSB phone operation, the occupied bandwidth extends downward from the suppressed carrier frequency, so transmitting on 3.601 MHz would cause part of the signal to fall outside the authorized phone subband. FCC rules require that all transmitted energy remain within the designated phone band segment.
llm.last_candidate: An Extra class operator may not answer the CQ of a station on 3.601 MHz LSB phone because the sideband components will extend beyond the edge of the 75-meter phone band segment.

questions.question_text: Who must be in physical control of the station apparatus of an amateur station aboard any vessel or craft that is documented or registered in the United States?
choices: Only a person with an FCC Marine Radio license grant
Only a person named in an amateur station license grant
Any person holding an FCC issued amateur license or who is authorized for alien reciprocal operation
Any person named in an amateur station license grant or a person holding an unrestricted Radiotelephone Operator Permit

llm.prose_fact: Any person holding an FCC issued amateur license or who is authorized for alien reciprocal operation must be in physical control of the station apparatus of an amateur station aboard any vessel or craft that is documented or registered in the United States.
llm.answer_explanation: FCC rules require that amateur stations on U.S.-documented or registered vessels be controlled by someone who is legally authorized to operate under U.S. amateur service regulations. This includes licensed U.S. amateurs and foreign amateurs operating under valid reciprocal operating authority.
llm.last_candidate: Any person holding an FCC issued amateur license or who is authorized for alien reciprocal operation must be in physical control of the station apparatus of an amateur station aboard any vessel or craft that is documented or registered in the United States.

questions.question_text: What is the required transmit frequency of a CW signal for channelized 60 meter operation?
choices: At the lowest frequency of the channel
At the center frequency of the channel
At the highest frequency of the channel
On any frequency where the signal’s sidebands are within the channel

llm.prose_fact: For channelized 60 meter operation using CW, the transmitter must be set to the exact center frequency of the assigned channel.
llm.answer_explanation: On the 60 meter band, FCC rules require all emissions, including CW, to be centered in the assigned channel to avoid interference and maintain the channelized structure. Tuning to the channel center frequency ensures compliance with the band’s specific channel-based allocation.
llm.last_candidate: For channelized 60 meter operation using CW, the transmitter must be set to the exact center frequency of the assigned channel.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum power permitted on the 2200-meter band?

llm.prose_fact: The maximum power permitted on the 2200-meter band is 1 watt EIRP (equivalent isotropic radiated power).
llm.answer_explanation: FCC rules limit amateur operation on the 2200-meter band to 1 watt EIRP to minimize potential interference to other services using this low-frequency spectrum. EIRP includes both transmitter power and antenna gain, so amateurs must ensure the total radiated power does not exceed this 1 watt limit.
llm.last_candidate: The maximum power permitted on the 2200-meter band is 1 watt EIRP (equivalent isotropic radiated power).

questions.question_text: If a station in a message forwarding system inadvertently forwards a message that is in violation of FCC rules, who is primarily accountable for the rules violation?
choices: The control operator of the packet bulletin board station
The control operator of the originating station
The control operators of all the stations in the system
The control operators of all the stations in the system not authenticating the source from which they accept communications

llm.prose_fact: If a station in a message forwarding system inadvertently forwards a message that violates FCC rules, the control operator of the originating station is primarily accountable for the rules violation.
llm.answer_explanation: FCC rules place primary responsibility for message content on the control operator of the station that originates the transmission. In a forwarding network, this means the originating station’s control operator is held chiefly accountable for any rule violations in the message.
llm.last_candidate: If a station in a message forwarding system inadvertently forwards a message that violates FCC rules, the control operator of the originating station is primarily accountable for the rules violation.

questions.question_text: Except in some parts of Alaska, what is the maximum power permitted on the 630-meter band?

llm.prose_fact: Except in some parts of Alaska, the maximum power permitted on the 630-meter band is 5 watts EIRP (equivalent isotropic radiated power).
llm.answer_explanation: The FCC limits amateur operation on the 630-meter band to 5 watts EIRP in most U.S. locations to control interference and ensure compatibility with other services. EIRP accounts for both transmitter output power and antenna gain, so operators must calculate their system to stay within this 5-watt EIRP limit.
llm.last_candidate: Except in some parts of Alaska, the maximum power permitted on the 630-meter band is 5 watts EIRP (equivalent isotropic radiated power).

questions.question_text: If an amateur station is installed aboard a ship or aircraft, what condition must be met before the station is operated?
choices: Its operation must be approved by the master of the ship or the pilot in command of the aircraft
The amateur station operator must agree not to transmit when the main radio of the ship or aircraft is in use
The amateur station must have a power supply that is completely independent of the main ship or aircraft power supply
The amateur station must operate only in specific segments of the amateur service HF and VHF bands

llm.prose_fact: If an amateur station is installed aboard a ship or aircraft, its operation must be approved by the master of the ship or the pilot in command of the aircraft.
llm.answer_explanation: The FCC requires that the person in overall command of the vessel or aircraft authorize amateur station operation to ensure safety and operational control. This keeps radio use coordinated with the ship’s or aircraft’s primary communications and safety responsibilities.
llm.last_candidate: If an amateur station is installed aboard a ship or aircraft, its operation must be approved by the master of the ship or the pilot in command of the aircraft.

questions.question_text: What licensing is required when operating an amateur station aboard a US-registered vessel in international waters?
choices: Any amateur license with an FCC Marine or Aircraft endorsement
Only General class or higher amateur licenses

llm.prose_fact: When operating an amateur station aboard a US-registered vessel in international waters, any FCC-issued amateur license is sufficient authorization.
llm.answer_explanation: For a US-registered vessel, FCC amateur service rules apply regardless of location, including international waters. Therefore, any valid FCC-issued amateur license grants the operator permission to use an amateur station on board.
llm.last_candidate: When operating an amateur station aboard a US-registered vessel in international waters, any FCC-issued amateur license is sufficient authorization.

questions.question_text: Which of the following constitutes a spurious emission?
choices: An amateur station transmission made without the proper call sign identification
A signal transmitted to prevent its detection by any station other than the intended recipient
Any transmitted signal that unintentionally interferes with another licensed radio station and whose levels exceed 40 dB below the fundamental power level
An emission outside the signal’s necessary bandwidth that can be reduced or eliminated without affecting the information transmitted

llm.prose_fact: A spurious emission is an emission outside the signal’s necessary bandwidth that can be reduced or eliminated without affecting the information transmitted.
llm.answer_explanation: In amateur radio, any signal energy that lies outside the bandwidth needed to carry the intended information and can be removed without degrading that information is considered spurious. Spurious emissions are unwanted and must be limited to comply with FCC technical standards and prevent interference to other users.
llm.last_candidate: A spurious emission is an emission outside the signal’s necessary bandwidth that can be reduced or eliminated without affecting the information transmitted.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is an acceptable bandwidth for digital voice or slow-scan TV transmissions made on the HF amateur bands?

llm.prose_fact: An acceptable bandwidth for digital voice or slow-scan TV transmissions made on the HF amateur bands is 3 kHz.
llm.answer_explanation: On the HF amateur bands, digital voice and slow-scan TV signals are expected to fit within a typical SSB channel, which is about 3 kHz wide. This bandwidth limit helps prevent interference and promotes efficient spectrum use as required by FCC rules for HF operation.
llm.last_candidate: An acceptable bandwidth for digital voice or slow-scan TV transmissions made on the HF amateur bands is 3 kHz.

questions.question_text: Within what distance must an amateur station protect an FCC monitoring facility from harmful interference?

llm.prose_fact: An amateur station must protect an FCC monitoring facility from harmful interference if it is located within 1 mile of that facility.
llm.answer_explanation: FCC monitoring stations perform sensitive signal measurements, so nearby amateur stations must avoid causing harmful interference. The 1 mile distance defines the protected zone around these facilities in the Amateur Extra rules.
llm.last_candidate: An amateur station must protect an FCC monitoring facility from harmful interference if it is located within 1 mile of that facility.

questions.question_text: What must the control operator of a repeater operating in the 70-centimeter band do if a radiolocation system experiences interference from that repeater?
choices: Reduce the repeater antenna HAAT (Height Above Average Terrain)
File an FAA NOTAM (Notice to Air Missions) with the repeater system's ERP, call sign, and six-character grid locator
Cease operation or make changes to the repeater that mitigate the interference

llm.prose_fact: If a radiolocation system experiences interference from a repeater operating in the 70-centimeter band, the control operator must cease operation or make changes to the repeater that mitigate the interference.
llm.answer_explanation: In the 70-centimeter band, amateur repeaters are secondary users and must not cause harmful interference to primary services such as radiolocation. Therefore, the control operator is required to stop operation or modify the repeater so that the interference is resolved.
llm.last_candidate: If a radiolocation system experiences interference from a repeater operating in the 70-centimeter band, the control operator must cease operation or make changes to the repeater that mitigate the interference.

questions.question_text: What is the National Radio Quiet Zone?
choices: An area surrounding the FCC monitoring station in Laurel, Maryland
An area in New Mexico surrounding the White Sands Test Area
An area surrounding the National Radio Astronomy Observatory
An area in Florida surrounding Cape Canaveral

llm.prose_fact: The National Radio Quiet Zone is an area surrounding the National Radio Astronomy Observatory.
llm.answer_explanation: Radio emissions are restricted in the National Radio Quiet Zone to protect the sensitive observations made by the National Radio Astronomy Observatory. This helps minimize radio frequency interference to important radio astronomy research facilities located there.
llm.last_candidate: The National Radio Quiet Zone is an area surrounding the National Radio Astronomy Observatory.

questions.question_text: Which of the following additional rules apply if you are erecting an amateur station antenna structure at a site at or near a public use airport?
choices: You may have to notify the Federal Aviation Administration and register it with the FCC as required by Part 17 of the FCC rules
You may have to enter the height above ground in meters, and the latitude and longitude in degrees, minutes, and seconds on the FAA website
You must file an Environmental Impact Statement with the EPA before construction begins
You must obtain a construction permit from the airport zoning authority per Part 119 of the FAA regulations

llm.prose_fact: If you are erecting an amateur station antenna structure at a site at or near a public use airport, you may have to notify the Federal Aviation Administration and register it with the FCC as required by Part 17 of the FCC rules.
llm.answer_explanation: Antenna structures near public use airports can pose potential hazards to air navigation, so the FAA and FCC require notice and registration under Part 17. This ensures the structure is properly evaluated, marked, and recorded for aviation safety and regulatory compliance.
llm.last_candidate: If you are erecting an amateur station antenna structure at a site at or near a public use airport, you may have to notify the Federal Aviation Administration and register it with the FCC as required by Part 17 of the FCC rules.

questions.question_text: To what type of regulations does PRB-1 apply?
choices: Use of wireless devices in vehicles

llm.prose_fact: PRB-1 applies to state and local zoning regulations that affect amateur radio antenna structures.
llm.answer_explanation: PRB-1 is an FCC policy requiring state and local governments to reasonably accommodate amateur radio antennas in their zoning and land-use regulations. It specifically addresses how these non-federal authorities regulate the placement and height of amateur radio antenna structures.
llm.last_candidate: PRB-1 applies to state and local zoning regulations that affect amateur radio antenna structures.

questions.question_text: What limitations may the FCC place on an amateur station if its signal causes interference to domestic broadcast reception, assuming that the receivers involved are of good engineering design?
choices: The amateur station must cease operation
The amateur station must cease operation on all frequencies below 30 MHz
The amateur station must cease operation on all frequencies above 30 MHz
The amateur station must avoid transmitting during certain hours on frequencies that cause the interference

llm.prose_fact: If an amateur station with a properly operating transmitter causes interference to domestic broadcast reception in receivers of good engineering design, the FCC may require that the amateur station avoid transmitting during certain hours on the specific frequencies that cause the interference.
llm.answer_explanation: The FCC can limit when an amateur station may operate on particular frequencies to prevent harmful interference to properly designed broadcast receivers. This protects domestic broadcast reception while still allowing amateur operation at other times or on other frequencies.
llm.last_candidate: If an amateur station with a properly operating transmitter causes interference to domestic broadcast reception in receivers of good engineering design, the FCC may require that the amateur station avoid transmitting during certain hours on the specific frequencies that cause the interference.

questions.question_text: Which amateur stations may be operated under RACES rules?
choices: Only those club stations licensed to Amateur Extra class operators
Any FCC-licensed amateur station except a Technician class
Any FCC-licensed amateur station certified by the responsible civil defense organization for the area served
Only stations meeting the FCC Part 97 technical standards for operation during an emergency

llm.prose_fact: Under RACES rules, any FCC-licensed amateur station certified by the responsible civil defense organization for the area served may be operated.
llm.answer_explanation: RACES operation is restricted to stations that are both FCC-licensed and specifically certified by the local civil defense authority. This ensures that emergency communications are coordinated and authorized within the served area during emergencies and civil defense activities.
llm.last_candidate: Under RACES rules, any FCC-licensed amateur station certified by the responsible civil defense organization for the area served may be operated.

questions.question_text: What frequencies are authorized to an amateur station operating under RACES rules?
choices: All amateur service frequencies authorized to the control operator
Specific segments in the amateur service MF, HF, VHF, and UHF bands

llm.prose_fact: Under RACES rules, an amateur station may operate on all amateur service frequencies that are authorized to the control operator.
llm.answer_explanation: RACES operation does not grant additional frequency privileges, so the station is limited to the same amateur bands and segments the control operator is already authorized to use. This ensures emergency communications stay within the operator’s existing license class privileges and FCC regulations.
llm.last_candidate: Under RACES rules, an amateur station may operate on all amateur service frequencies that are authorized to the control operator.

questions.question_text: What does PRB-1 require of state and local regulations affecting amateur radio antenna size and structures?
choices: No limitations may be placed on antenna size or placement
Reasonable accommodations of amateur radio must be made
Such structures must be permitted when use for emergency communications can be demonstrated
Such structures must be permitted if certified by a registered professional engineer

llm.prose_fact: PRB-1 requires that state and local regulations affecting amateur radio antenna size and structures must make reasonable accommodations for amateur radio communications and must not preclude amateur service communications altogether.
llm.answer_explanation: Under PRB-1, the FCC directs state and local governments to reasonably accommodate amateur radio operations when regulating antennas. This protects the ability of licensed amateurs to communicate while still allowing local authorities to enforce legitimate zoning and safety regulations.
llm.last_candidate: PRB-1 requires that state and local regulations affecting amateur radio antenna size and structures must make reasonable accommodations for amateur radio communications and must not preclude amateur service communications altogether.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum bandwidth for a data emission on 60 meters?

llm.prose_fact: The maximum bandwidth for a data emission on the 60 meter amateur band is 2.8 kHz.
llm.answer_explanation: On 60 meters, the FCC limits data emissions to a maximum bandwidth of 2.8 kHz to ensure compatibility and prevent interference within the shared channels. Amateur Extra operators must observe this 2.8 kHz limit when configuring digital modes on 60 meters.
llm.last_candidate: The maximum bandwidth for a data emission on the 60 meter amateur band is 2.8 kHz.

questions.question_text: Which of the following apply to communications transmitted to amateur stations in foreign countries?
choices: Third party traffic must be limited to that intended for the exclusive use of government and non-Government Organization (NGOs) involved in emergency relief activities
All transmissions must be in English
Communications must be limited to those incidental to the purpose of the amateur service and remarks of a personal nature

llm.prose_fact: Communications transmitted to amateur stations in foreign countries must be limited to those incidental to the purpose of the amateur service and remarks of a personal nature.
llm.answer_explanation: FCC rules and international agreements restrict third-party and international amateur communications to content that supports the amateur service’s technical and operational purposes plus personal, noncommercial conversation. This maintains the noncommercial, self-training, and hobby nature of amateur radio across borders.
llm.last_candidate: Communications transmitted to amateur stations in foreign countries must be limited to those incidental to the purpose of the amateur service and remarks of a personal nature.

questions.question_text: How long must an operator wait after filing a notification with the Utilities Technology Council (UTC) before operating on the 2200-meter or 630-meter band?
choices: Operators must not operate until approval is received
Operators may operate after 30 days, providing they have not been told that their station is within 1 kilometer of PLC systems using those frequencies
Operators may not operate until a test signal has been transmitted in coordination with the local power company
Operations may commence immediately, and may continue unless interference is reported by the UTC

llm.prose_fact: An amateur operator may begin operating on the 2200-meter or 630-meter band 30 days after filing a notification with the Utilities Technology Council (UTC), provided they have not been informed that their station is within 1 kilometer of PLC systems using those frequencies.
llm.answer_explanation: The 30-day waiting period allows time for the UTC to determine whether nearby power line carrier (PLC) systems could be affected. If no notice of being within 1 kilometer of PLC operations on those frequencies is received, operation on 2200 meters or 630 meters may begin.
llm.last_candidate: An amateur operator may begin operating on the 2200-meter or 630-meter band 30 days after filing a notification with the Utilities Technology Council (UTC), provided they have not been informed that their station is within 1 kilometer of PLC systems using those frequencies.

questions.question_text: What is an IARP?
choices: A permit that allows US amateurs to operate in certain countries of the Americas
The internal amateur radio practices policy of the FCC
An indication of increased antenna reflected power
A forecast of intermittent aurora radio propagation

llm.prose_fact: An International Amateur Radio Permit (IARP) is a permit that allows U.S. amateur radio operators to operate their stations in certain countries of the Americas without obtaining an individual reciprocal license from each country.
llm.answer_explanation: The IARP is an agreed-upon permit among certain nations in the Americas that recognizes a U.S. amateur’s operating privileges when traveling. It simplifies temporary operation abroad by serving as a standardized authorization in participating countries.
llm.last_candidate: An International Amateur Radio Permit (IARP) is a permit that allows U.S. amateur radio operators to operate their stations in certain countries of the Americas without obtaining an individual reciprocal license from each country.

questions.question_text: Under what situation may a station transmit third party communications while being automatically controlled?
choices: Only when transmitting RTTY or data emissions
Only when transmitting SSB or CW
On any mode approved by the National Telecommunication and Information Administration

llm.prose_fact: A station may transmit third party communications while being automatically controlled only when transmitting RTTY or data emissions.
llm.answer_explanation: FCC rules for automatically controlled stations restrict third party communications to RTTY or data modes to ensure predictable, regulated operation without a control operator present in real time. This limitation helps maintain control and accountability for message content in unattended digital operation on the amateur bands.
llm.last_candidate: A station may transmit third party communications while being automatically controlled only when transmitting RTTY or data emissions.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is required in order to operate in accordance with CEPT rules in foreign countries where permitted?
choices: You must identify in the official language of the country in which you are operating
The US embassy must approve of your operation
You must have a copy of FCC Public Notice DA 16-1048
You must append "/CEPT" to your call sign

llm.prose_fact: To operate in accordance with CEPT rules in foreign countries where permitted, you must have a copy of FCC Public Notice DA 16-1048.
llm.answer_explanation: FCC Public Notice DA 16-1048 implements the CEPT amateur radio license arrangements for U.S. amateurs and serves as your proof of operating authority when visiting participating countries. Carrying a copy of this document shows foreign regulators that you are authorized to operate under CEPT rules.
llm.last_candidate: To operate in accordance with CEPT rules in foreign countries where permitted, you must have a copy of FCC Public Notice DA 16-1048.

questions.question_text: What notifications must be given before transmitting on the 630- or 2200-meter bands?
choices: A special endorsement must be requested from the FCC
An environmental impact statement must be filed with the Department of the Interior
Operators must inform the FAA of their intent to operate, giving their call sign and distance to the nearest runway
Operators must inform the Utilities Technology Council (UTC) of their call sign and coordinates of the station

llm.prose_fact: Before transmitting on the 630- or 2200-meter amateur bands, operators must inform the Utilities Technology Council (UTC) of their call sign and the coordinates of the station.
llm.answer_explanation: Notification to the Utilities Technology Council (UTC) with your call sign and station coordinates is required so that potential interference to power line carrier systems and utility infrastructure can be evaluated. This coordination step is part of the FCC rules specific to operation on the 630- and 2200-meter bands.
llm.last_candidate: Before transmitting on the 630- or 2200-meter amateur bands, operators must inform the Utilities Technology Council (UTC) of their call sign and the coordinates of the station.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum permissible duration of a remotely controlled station’s transmissions if its control link malfunctions?

llm.prose_fact: If the control link of a remotely controlled amateur station malfunctions, the maximum permissible duration of the station’s transmissions is 3 minutes.
llm.answer_explanation: FCC rules limit a remotely controlled station to no more than 3 minutes of transmitting after control is lost to prevent unchecked, potentially interfering signals. This time limit helps ensure that malfunctioning or uncontrolled transmissions do not continue indefinitely on the air.
llm.last_candidate: If the control link of a remotely controlled amateur station malfunctions, the maximum permissible duration of the station’s transmissions is 3 minutes.

questions.question_text: What is the highest modulation index permitted at the highest modulation frequency for angle modulation below 29.0 MHz?

llm.prose_fact: For angle modulation emissions below 29.0 MHz, the highest permitted modulation index at the highest modulation frequency is 1.0.
llm.answer_explanation: FCC rules limit the modulation index for angle modulation below 29.0 MHz to 1.0 at the highest modulation frequency to control occupied bandwidth and interference. This ensures the signal remains within its authorized emission limits for Amateur Extra operators.
llm.last_candidate: For angle modulation emissions below 29.0 MHz, the highest permitted modulation index at the highest modulation frequency is 1.0.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum mean power level for a spurious emission below 30 MHz with respect to the fundamental emission?

llm.prose_fact: The maximum mean power level for a spurious emission below 30 MHz is -43 dB with respect to the fundamental emission.
llm.answer_explanation: FCC rules limit spurious emissions to -43 dB below the carrier to prevent interference on adjacent and nearby frequencies. This ensures that unwanted emissions are much weaker than the intended signal while operating below 30 MHz.
llm.last_candidate: The maximum mean power level for a spurious emission below 30 MHz is -43 dB with respect to the fundamental emission.

questions.question_text: Which of the following operating arrangements allows an FCC-licensed US citizen to operate in many European countries, and amateurs from many European countries to operate in the US?

llm.prose_fact: The operating arrangement that allows an FCC-licensed US citizen to operate in many European countries, and amateurs from many European countries to operate in the US, is the CEPT agreement.
llm.answer_explanation: CEPT is an international reciprocal operating agreement that standardizes temporary amateur radio operating privileges among member countries. Under CEPT, eligible US and European amateurs can legally operate in each other’s countries without obtaining an additional local amateur license.
llm.last_candidate: The operating arrangement that allows an FCC-licensed US citizen to operate in many European countries, and amateurs from many European countries to operate in the US, is the CEPT agreement.

questions.question_text: In what portion of the 630-meter band are phone emissions permitted?

llm.prose_fact: In the 630-meter band, phone emissions are permitted over the entire band.
llm.answer_explanation: FCC rules allow voice (phone) operation anywhere within the authorized 630-meter amateur band segment. This means there is no sub-band restriction for phone emissions on 630 meters for Amateur Extra licensees.
llm.last_candidate: In the 630-meter band, phone emissions are permitted over the entire band.

questions.question_text: What is the definition of telemetry?
choices: One-way transmission of measurements at a distance from the measuring instrument
Two-way transmissions in excess of 1000 feet
One-way transmission that initiates, modifies, or terminates the functions of a device at a distance

llm.prose_fact: Telemetry is defined as the one-way transmission of measurements at a distance from the measuring instrument.
llm.answer_explanation: In the Amateur Extra exam context, telemetry refers specifically to sending measurement or status data in one direction from the remote instrument back to a control or monitoring point. This definition distinguishes it from two-way control or voice communications.
llm.last_candidate: Telemetry is defined as the one-way transmission of measurements at a distance from the measuring instrument.

questions.question_text: Which of the following may transmit encrypted messages?
choices: Telecommand signals from a space telecommand station
Auxiliary relay links carrying repeater audio

llm.prose_fact: Telecommand signals from a space telecommand station may transmit encrypted messages under the FCC Amateur Extra rules.
llm.answer_explanation: The FCC rules specifically allow encryption when it is used for telecommand of a space station or radio control from a space telecommand station. This exception permits secure control of satellites while maintaining compliance with amateur service regulations.
llm.last_candidate: Telecommand signals from a space telecommand station may transmit encrypted messages under the FCC Amateur Extra rules.

questions.question_text: What is a space telecommand station?
choices: An amateur station located on the surface of the Earth for communication with other Earth stations by means of Earth satellites
An amateur station that transmits communications to initiate, modify, or terminate functions of a space station
An amateur station located in a satellite or a balloon more than 50 kilometers above the surface of the Earth
An amateur station that receives telemetry from a satellite or balloon more than 50 kilometers above the surface of the Earth

llm.prose_fact: A space telecommand station is an amateur station that transmits communications to initiate, modify, or terminate functions of a space station.
llm.answer_explanation: In the Amateur Extra exam context, a space telecommand station specifically controls the operation of an amateur space station by sending signals that start, change, or stop its functions. This definition aligns with FCC rules governing amateur operation of satellites and other space-based stations.
llm.last_candidate: A space telecommand station is an amateur station that transmits communications to initiate, modify, or terminate functions of a space station.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is required in the identification transmissions from a balloon-borne telemetry station?
choices: The output power of the balloon transmitter
The station's six-character Maidenhead grid locator

llm.prose_fact: Identification transmissions from a balloon-borne telemetry station must include the station's FCC-assigned call sign.
llm.answer_explanation: The FCC requires all amateur stations, including balloon-borne telemetry stations, to identify with their call sign so the transmitting station can be clearly and uniquely identified. Including the call sign in the telemetry identification satisfies this regulatory requirement for proper station identification on the Amateur Extra exam.
llm.last_candidate: Identification transmissions from a balloon-borne telemetry station must include the station's FCC-assigned call sign.

questions.question_text: What must be posted at the location of a station being operated by telecommand on or within 50 kilometers of the Earth’s surface?
choices: A photocopy of the station license
A label with the name, address, and telephone number of the station licensee
A label with the name, address, and telephone number of the control operator

llm.prose_fact: When a station is being operated by telecommand on or within 50 kilometers of the Earth’s surface, the posting at the station location must include the FCC licensee’s name, the station call sign, and the address and telephone number of the station licensee or designated control point, since all these choices are correct.
llm.answer_explanation: For telecommanded stations near the Earth's surface, the rules require clear identification of the licensee and how to reach the control point. Listing the name, call sign, and contact information ensures accountability and the ability to promptly control or shut down the station if necessary.
llm.last_candidate: When a station is being operated by telecommand on or within 50 kilometers of the Earth’s surface, the posting at the station location must include the FCC licensee’s name, the station call sign, and the address and telephone number of the station licensee or designated control point, since all these choices are correct.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum permitted transmitter output power when operating a model craft by telecommand?

llm.prose_fact: The maximum permitted transmitter output power when operating a model craft by telecommand is 1 watt.

llm.last_candidate: The maximum permitted transmitter output power when operating a model craft by telecommand is 1 watt.

questions.question_text: Which of the following HF amateur bands include allocations for space stations?
choices: Satellite operation is permitted on all HF bands

llm.last_candidate: The HF amateur bands that include allocations for space stations are 40 meters, 20 meters, 15 meters, and 10 meters.

questions.question_text: Which UHF amateur bands have frequencies authorized for space stations?

llm.last_candidate: The UHF amateur bands that have frequencies authorized for space stations are 70 centimeters and 13 centimeters.

questions.question_text: Which amateur stations are eligible to be telecommand stations of space stations, subject to the privileges of the class of operator license held by the control operator of the station?

llm.last_candidate: Any amateur station so designated by the space station licensee is eligible to be the telecommand station of an amateur space station, subject to the privileges of the class of operator license held by the control operator.

questions.question_text: Which amateur stations are eligible to operate as Earth stations?
choices: Any amateur licensee who has successfully completed the AMSAT space communications course
Only those of General, Advanced or Amateur Extra class operators
Only those of Amateur Extra class operators
Any amateur station, subject to the privileges of the class of operator license held by the control operator

The license class determines where and how that Earth station operation may occur on the amateur bands, not whether it is allowed at all.
llm.last_candidate: Any amateur station is eligible to operate as an Earth station, subject to the privileges of the class of operator license held by the control operator.

questions.question_text: Which of the following amateur stations may transmit one-way communications?
choices: A space station, beacon station, or telecommand station
A local repeater or linked repeater station
A message forwarding station or automatically controlled digital station

llm.last_candidate: One-way communications are permitted from an amateur space station, beacon station, or telecommand station.

questions.question_text: For which types of out-of-pocket expenses do the Part 97 rules state that VEs and VECs may be reimbursed?
choices: Preparing, processing, administering, and coordinating an examination for an amateur radio operator license
Teaching an amateur operator license examination preparation course
No expenses are authorized for reimbursement
Providing amateur operator license examination preparation training materials

questions.question_text: Who is tasked by Part 97 with maintaining the pools of questions for all US amateur license examinations?

questions.question_text: What must the VE team do with the application form if the examinee does not pass the exam?

Return the application document to the examinee
Send the application form to the FCC and inform the FCC of the grade

llm.last_candidate: If an examinee does not pass the exam, the Volunteer Examiner (VE) team must return the application document to the examinee.

questions.question_text: Who is responsible for the proper conduct and necessary supervision during an amateur operator license examination session?

llm.last_candidate: Each administering VE is responsible for the proper conduct and necessary supervision during an amateur operator license examination session.

questions.question_text: What should a VE do if a candidate fails to comply with the examiner’s instructions during an amateur operator license examination?
choices: Warn the candidate that continued failure to comply will result in termination of the examination
Allow the candidate to complete the examination, but invalidate the results
Immediately terminate everyone’s examination and close the session

llm.last_candidate: If a candidate fails to comply with the examiner’s instructions during an amateur operator license examination, the Volunteer Examiner (VE) must immediately terminate the candidate’s examination.

questions.question_text: To which of the following examinees may a VE not administer an examination?

questions.question_text: What may be the penalty for a VE who fraudulently administers or certifies an examination?

A fine of up to $1,000 per occurrence
A sentence of up to one year in prison

llm.last_candidate: The penalty for a VE who fraudulently administers or certifies an examination may be revocation of the VE’s amateur station license grant and the suspension of the VE’s amateur operator license grant.

questions.question_text: What must the administering VEs do after the administration of a successful examination for an amateur operator license?

They must collect and submit the documents to the coordinating VEC for grading
They must submit the application document to the coordinating VEC according to the coordinating VEC instructions
They must return the documents to the applicant for submission to the FCC according to the FCC instructions

llm.last_candidate: After the administration of a successful examination for an amateur operator license, the administering VEs must submit the application document to the coordinating VEC according to the coordinating VEC instructions.

questions.question_text: What must the VE team do if an examinee scores a passing grade on all examination elements needed for an upgrade or new license?

Three VEs must certify that the examinee is qualified for the license grant and that they have complied with the administering VE requirements

llm.last_candidate: If an examinee earns a passing grade on all examination elements required for an upgrade or new amateur radio license, three volunteer examiners (VEs) must certify that the examinee is qualified for the license grant and that they have complied with all administering VE requirements.

questions.question_text: On what frequencies are spread spectrum transmissions permitted?

Only on amateur frequencies above 222 MHz
Only on amateur frequencies above 420 MHz
Only on amateur frequencies above 144 MHz

questions.question_text: What privileges are authorized in the US to persons holding an amateur service license granted by the government of Canada?

llm.last_candidate: In the United States, a person holding an amateur service license granted by the government of Canada is authorized to operate under the terms and conditions of their Canadian amateur service license, but their privileges may not exceed those of a US Amateur Extra class license.

questions.question_text: Under what circumstances may a dealer sell an external RF power amplifier capable of operation below 144 MHz if it has not been granted FCC certification?

llm.last_candidate: A dealer may sell an external RF power amplifier capable of operation below 144 MHz if it has not been granted FCC certification only when the amplifier is constructed or modified by an amateur radio operator for use at an amateur station.

questions.question_text: Which of the following geographic descriptions approximately describes "Line A"?

A line roughly parallel to and west of the US Atlantic coastline

questions.question_text: Amateur stations may not transmit in which of the following frequency segments if they are located in the contiguous 48 states and north of Line A?

To allow use of a special event call sign
To allow a VE group with less than three VEs to administer examinations in a remote, sparsely populated area

questions.question_text: When may an amateur station send a message to a business?

When the pecuniary interest of the amateur or his or her employer is less than $50

llm.last_candidate: An amateur station may send a message to a business only when neither the amateur nor their employer has any pecuniary interest in the communications.

questions.question_text: Which of the following types of amateur station communications are prohibited?

questions.question_text: Which of the following cannot be transmitted over an amateur radio mesh network?
choices: Messages encoded to obscure their meaning

questions.question_text: Who may be the control operator of an auxiliary station?
choices: Only Technician, General, Advanced, or Amateur Extra class operators
Only General, Advanced, or Amateur Extra class operators

llm.prose_fact: Only Technician, General, Advanced, or Amateur Extra class operators may be the control operator of an auxiliary station.
llm.answer_explanation: FCC rules require that the control operator of an auxiliary station hold at least a Technician class license. This ensures that the operator has the necessary privileges and knowledge to properly control unattended or remotely controlled transmitting equipment used in auxiliary operation.
llm.last_candidate: Only Technician, General, Advanced, or Amateur Extra class operators may be the control operator of an auxiliary station.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes one of the standards that must be met by an external RF power amplifier if it is to qualify for a grant of FCC certification?
choices: It must produce full legal output when driven by not more than 5 watts of mean RF input power
It must have received an Underwriters Laboratory certification for electrical safety as well as having met IEEE standard 14.101(B)
It must exhibit a gain of less than 23 dB when driven by 10 watts or less
It must satisfy the FCC’s spurious emission standards when operated at the lesser of 1500 watts or its full output power

llm.prose_fact: To qualify for a grant of FCC certification, an external RF power amplifier must satisfy the FCC’s spurious emission standards when operated at the lesser of 1500 watts or its full output power.
llm.answer_explanation: FCC certification requires that external RF power amplifiers limit unwanted emissions to protect other spectrum users. Testing at the lesser of 1500 watts or the amplifier’s full output power ensures compliance under maximum normal operating conditions for amateur stations.
llm.last_candidate: To qualify for a grant of FCC certification, an external RF power amplifier must satisfy the FCC’s spurious emission standards when operated at the lesser of 1500 watts or its full output power.

questions.question_text: What is the direction of an ascending pass for an amateur satellite?

llm.prose_fact: The direction of an ascending pass for an amateur satellite is from south to north.
llm.answer_explanation: An ascending pass is defined as the portion of the satellite’s orbit where it moves from the Southern Hemisphere toward the Northern Hemisphere as seen from Earth. For an observer, this corresponds to the satellite traveling generally from south to north across the sky.
llm.last_candidate: The direction of an ascending pass for an amateur satellite is from south to north.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is characteristic of an inverting linear transponder?
choices: Doppler shift is reduced because the uplink and downlink shifts are in opposite directions
Signal position in the band is reversed
Upper sideband on the uplink becomes lower sideband on the downlink, and vice versa

llm.prose_fact: An inverting linear transponder produces an output signal that is the conjugate of the input, reverses the sideband orientation (upper to lower and lower to upper), and reverses the frequency spectrum of the transmitted passband, so all these choices are correct.
llm.answer_explanation: Each listed behavior describes how an inverting linear transponder processes signals in amateur satellite operation. Because it inverts both sideband sense and passband frequency while maintaining linearity, all of the provided statements are simultaneously true.
llm.last_candidate: An inverting linear transponder produces an output signal that is the conjugate of the input, reverses the sideband orientation (upper to lower and lower to upper), and reverses the frequency spectrum of the transmitted passband, so all these choices are correct.

questions.question_text: How is an upload signal processed by an inverting linear transponder?
choices: The signal is detected and remodulated on the reverse sideband
The signal is passed through a nonlinear filter
The signal is reduced to I and Q components, and the Q component is filtered out
The signal is mixed with a local oscillator signal and the difference product is transmitted

llm.prose_fact: In an inverting linear transponder, the upload signal is mixed with a local oscillator signal and the difference product is transmitted.
llm.answer_explanation: An inverting linear transponder uses frequency mixing so that the uplink signal is converted to a different frequency by subtracting it from a local oscillator signal. This difference product is then amplified and retransmitted, preserving the modulation while shifting the frequency and inverting the spectrum as used in satellite and similar amateur applications.
llm.last_candidate: In an inverting linear transponder, the upload signal is mixed with a local oscillator signal and the difference product is transmitted.

questions.question_text: What is meant by the “mode” of an amateur radio satellite?
choices: Whether the satellite is in a low earth or geostationary orbit
The satellite’s uplink and downlink frequency bands
The satellite’s orientation with respect to the Earth
Whether the satellite is in a polar or equatorial orbit

llm.prose_fact: The “mode” of an amateur radio satellite is the designation of its uplink and downlink frequency bands.
llm.answer_explanation: In amateur satellite operation, the mode label tells operators which frequency band is used for transmitting to the satellite (uplink) and which band is used for receiving from it (downlink). This lets operators choose appropriate equipment and frequencies for successful communication through the satellite.
llm.last_candidate: The “mode” of an amateur radio satellite is the designation of its uplink and downlink frequency bands.

questions.question_text: What do the letters in a satellite’s mode designator specify?
choices: Power limits for uplink and downlink transmissions
The location of the ground control station
The polarization of uplink and downlink signals
The uplink and downlink frequency ranges

llm.prose_fact: In a satellite’s mode designator, the letters specify the uplink and downlink frequency ranges.
llm.answer_explanation: Each letter in the satellite mode (such as Mode U/V or L/S) corresponds to a specific amateur band used for the uplink and the downlink. This lets operators know which frequency ranges to use when communicating through that satellite.
llm.last_candidate: In a satellite’s mode designator, the letters specify the uplink and downlink frequency ranges.

questions.question_text: What are Keplerian elements?
choices: Parameters that define the orbit of a satellite
Phase reversing elements in a Yagi antenna
High-emission heater filaments used in magnetron tubes
Encrypting codes used for spread spectrum modulation

llm.prose_fact: Keplerian elements are parameters that define the orbit of a satellite.
llm.answer_explanation: In amateur satellite operation, Keplerian elements mathematically describe a satellite's orbit so tracking software can predict its position over time. These orbital parameters are essential for knowing when and where to point antennas and when communication windows will occur.
llm.last_candidate: Keplerian elements are parameters that define the orbit of a satellite.

questions.question_text: Which of the following types of signals can be relayed through a linear transponder?

llm.prose_fact: A linear transponder can relay single sideband voice, FM, CW, RTTY, PSK, and other digital signals as long as they all fit within its passband.
llm.answer_explanation: A linear transponder simply translates a range of input frequencies to a different output range, so any mode that occupies bandwidth within that passband can be relayed. This allows multiple analog and digital signal types to be used simultaneously on the same satellite transponder.
llm.last_candidate: A linear transponder can relay single sideband voice, FM, CW, RTTY, PSK, and other digital signals as long as they all fit within its passband.

questions.question_text: Why should effective radiated power (ERP) be limited to a satellite that uses a linear transponder?
choices: To prevent creating errors in the satellite telemetry
To avoid reducing the downlink power to all other users
To prevent the satellite from emitting out-of-band signals
To avoid interfering with terrestrial QSOs

llm.prose_fact: Effective radiated power (ERP) should be limited to a satellite that uses a linear transponder to avoid reducing the downlink power to all other users.
llm.answer_explanation: A linear transponder has a fixed total output power, so if one station transmits with excessive ERP, it forces the transponder to reduce power available to other signals. Limiting ERP helps ensure fair and reliable downlink signals for all users sharing the satellite transponder.
llm.last_candidate: Effective radiated power (ERP) should be limited to a satellite that uses a linear transponder to avoid reducing the downlink power to all other users.

questions.question_text: What do the terms “L band” and “S band” specify?

llm.prose_fact: In amateur radio satellite and microwave operation, the terms “L band” and “S band” specify the 23- and 13-centimeter bands, respectively.
llm.answer_explanation: On the Amateur Extra exam, “L band” and “S band” are defined by their corresponding amateur microwave allocations, which are the 23-centimeter and 13-centimeter bands. Knowing these band designations is important for understanding satellite and microwave operating practices and equipment.
llm.last_candidate: In amateur radio satellite and microwave operation, the terms “L band” and “S band” specify the 23- and 13-centimeter bands, respectively.

questions.question_text: What type of satellite appears to stay in one position in the sky?

llm.prose_fact: A geostationary satellite appears to stay in one fixed position in the sky relative to an observer on Earth.
llm.answer_explanation: A geostationary satellite orbits Earth at the equator with the same rotational period as Earth, so it appears motionless in the sky. This fixed apparent position makes it easy for amateur radio operators to point antennas and maintain continuous communication links.
llm.last_candidate: A geostationary satellite appears to stay in one fixed position in the sky relative to an observer on Earth.

questions.question_text: What type of antenna can be used to minimize the effects of spin modulation and Faraday rotation?

llm.prose_fact: A circularly polarized antenna can be used to minimize the effects of spin modulation and Faraday rotation.
llm.answer_explanation: Circular polarization is less sensitive to changes in signal polarization caused by satellite spin and ionospheric Faraday rotation, so received signal strength varies less. This makes circularly polarized antennas especially useful for satellite and space communication links tested on the Amateur Extra exam.
llm.last_candidate: A circularly polarized antenna can be used to minimize the effects of spin modulation and Faraday rotation.

questions.question_text: In digital television, what does a coding rate of 3/4 mean?
choices: 25% of the data sent is forward error correction data
Data compression reduces data rate by 3/4
1/4 of the time interval is used as a guard interval
Three, four-bit words are used to transmit each pixel

llm.prose_fact: In digital television, a coding rate of 3/4 means that 25% of the data sent is forward error correction data.
llm.answer_explanation: A coding rate of 3/4 indicates that three parts are payload data and one part is error correction, so one quarter (25%) of the transmitted bits are used for forward error correction. This balance improves signal reliability for digital TV while keeping most of the bandwidth available for program content.
llm.last_candidate: In digital television, a coding rate of 3/4 means that 25% of the data sent is forward error correction data.

questions.question_text: How many horizontal lines make up a fast-scan (NTSC) television frame?

llm.prose_fact: A fast-scan NTSC television frame is made up of 525 horizontal lines.
llm.answer_explanation: In standard analog NTSC television, the video signal format is defined to contain 525 horizontal scan lines per frame. Amateur fast-scan TV uses this same NTSC standard, so each frame consists of 525 lines.
llm.last_candidate: A fast-scan NTSC television frame is made up of 525 horizontal lines.

questions.question_text: How is an interlaced scanning pattern generated in a fast-scan (NTSC) television system?
choices: By scanning each field from bottom-to-top
By scanning lines from left-to-right in one field and right-to-left in the next
By scanning odd-numbered lines in one field and even-numbered lines in the next

llm.prose_fact: In a fast-scan NTSC television system, an interlaced scanning pattern is generated by scanning the odd-numbered lines in one field and the even-numbered lines in the next field.
llm.answer_explanation: NTSC interlaced scanning divides each video frame into two fields, one containing all the odd lines and the other all the even lines. This approach effectively doubles the perceived refresh rate and reduces flicker in the displayed image, which is important for fast-scan amateur television signals.
llm.last_candidate: In a fast-scan NTSC television system, an interlaced scanning pattern is generated by scanning the odd-numbered lines in one field and the even-numbered lines in the next field.

questions.question_text: How is color information sent in analog SSTV?
choices: Color information is sent on a 2.8 kHz subcarrier
Color is sent in a color burst at the end of each line
Color is amplitude modulated on the frequency modulated intensity signal

llm.prose_fact: In analog SSTV, color information is sent by transmitting the separate color lines sequentially.
llm.answer_explanation: Analog SSTV encodes each line of the image one after another, including its color components, so the receiving station can reconstruct the full color picture line by line. This sequential transmission of color lines is fundamental to how analog SSTV images are built up over time in amateur radio.
llm.last_candidate: In analog SSTV, color information is sent by transmitting the separate color lines sequentially.

questions.question_text: Which of the following describes the use of vestigial sideband in analog fast-scan TV transmissions?
choices: The vestigial sideband carries the audio information
The vestigial sideband contains chroma information
Vestigial sideband reduces the bandwidth while increasing the fidelity of low frequency video components
Vestigial sideband provides high frequency emphasis to sharpen the picture

llm.prose_fact: In analog fast-scan TV transmissions, vestigial sideband reduces the bandwidth while increasing the fidelity of low frequency video components.
llm.answer_explanation: Vestigial sideband TV transmission partially suppresses one sideband to narrow the occupied spectrum while preserving enough of it to accurately reproduce low-frequency video information. This allows efficient use of bandwidth without degrading important picture detail in the baseband video signal.
llm.last_candidate: In analog fast-scan TV transmissions, vestigial sideband reduces the bandwidth while increasing the fidelity of low frequency video components.

questions.question_text: What is vestigial sideband modulation?
choices: Amplitude modulation in which one complete sideband and a portion of the other are transmitted
A type of modulation in which one sideband is inverted
Narrow-band FM modulation achieved by filtering one sideband from the audio before frequency modulating the carrier
Spread spectrum modulation achieved by applying FM modulation following single sideband amplitude modulation

llm.prose_fact: Vestigial sideband modulation is amplitude modulation in which one complete sideband and a portion of the other are transmitted.
llm.answer_explanation: In vestigial sideband, most of one sideband is transmitted along with a reduced portion of the other, conserving bandwidth while maintaining sufficient information for proper demodulation. This technique is commonly used in television transmission and is relevant to understanding advanced amplitude modulation methods on the Amateur Extra exam.
llm.last_candidate: Vestigial sideband modulation is amplitude modulation in which one complete sideband and a portion of the other are transmitted.

questions.question_text: Which types of modulation are used for amateur television DVB-T signals?

llm.prose_fact: Amateur television DVB-T signals use QAM and QPSK modulation.
llm.answer_explanation: Digital Video Broadcasting - Terrestrial (DVB-T) in the amateur service relies on quadrature amplitude modulation (QAM) and quadrature phase shift keying (QPSK) as its primary digital modulation schemes. These modulation types efficiently carry digital video and audio data within the allocated amateur television bandwidth.
llm.last_candidate: Amateur television DVB-T signals use QAM and QPSK modulation.

questions.question_text: What technique allows commercial analog TV receivers to be used for fast-scan TV operations on the 70-centimeter band?
choices: Transmitting on channels shared with cable TV
Transmitting on the abandoned TV channel 2
Using USB and demodulating the signal with a computer sound card

llm.prose_fact: Commercial analog TV receivers can be used for fast-scan TV operations on the 70-centimeter band by transmitting on channels shared with cable TV.
llm.answer_explanation: Cable TV channel frequencies can fall within or near the 70-centimeter amateur band, allowing standard analog TV receivers to display amateur fast-scan TV signals. By using these shared channel frequencies, hams can transmit ATV without requiring specialized receiving equipment beyond a typical analog TV set connected appropriately.
llm.last_candidate: Commercial analog TV receivers can be used for fast-scan TV operations on the 70-centimeter band by transmitting on channels shared with cable TV.

questions.question_text: What kind of receiver can be used to receive and decode SSTV using the Digital Radio Mondiale (DRM) protocol?

llm.prose_fact: An SSB receiver can be used to receive and decode slow-scan television (SSTV) using the Digital Radio Mondiale (DRM) protocol.
llm.answer_explanation: DRM-based SSTV signals are transmitted as complex digital waveforms that fit within an SSB channel, so an SSB receiver can properly tune and pass the baseband signal to a computer or decoder. In the Amateur Extra exam context, recognizing that SSB provides the needed bandwidth and fidelity for DRM demodulation is essential.
llm.last_candidate: An SSB receiver can be used to receive and decode slow-scan television (SSTV) using the Digital Radio Mondiale (DRM) protocol.

questions.question_text: What aspect of an analog slow-scan television signal encodes the brightness of the picture?

llm.prose_fact: In an analog slow-scan television (SSTV) signal, the brightness of the picture is encoded by the tone frequency.
llm.answer_explanation: SSTV transmits video information by varying the audio tone frequency to represent different brightness levels for each point in the image. The receiver decodes these tone frequencies to reconstruct the picture’s grayscale or color intensity for the Amateur Extra exam context.
llm.last_candidate: In an analog slow-scan television (SSTV) signal, the brightness of the picture is encoded by the tone frequency.

questions.question_text: What is the function of the vertical interval signaling (VIS) code sent as part of an SSTV transmission?
choices: To lock the color burst oscillator in color SSTV images
To identify the SSTV mode being used
To identify the call sign of the station transmitting

llm.prose_fact: In an SSTV transmission, the vertical interval signaling (VIS) code functions to identify the SSTV mode being used.
llm.answer_explanation: The VIS code tells the receiving equipment which SSTV mode to decode so that the image can be properly reconstructed. This automatic mode identification is essential for correct demodulation and display in Amateur Radio SSTV operation.
llm.last_candidate: In an SSTV transmission, the vertical interval signaling (VIS) code functions to identify the SSTV mode being used.

questions.question_text: What signals SSTV receiving software to begin a new picture line?

llm.prose_fact: In slow-scan television (SSTV), specific tone frequencies signal the receiving software to begin a new picture line.
llm.answer_explanation: SSTV encodes line timing information as distinct audio tone frequencies, which the receiving software recognizes as the cue to start decoding and displaying a new line of the image. This tone-based timing method keeps transmitting and receiving stations synchronized for proper picture reconstruction.
llm.last_candidate: In slow-scan television (SSTV), specific tone frequencies signal the receiving software to begin a new picture line.

questions.question_text: What indicator is required to be used by US-licensed operators when operating a station via remote control and the remote transmitter is located in the US?
choices: / followed by the USPS two-letter abbreviation for the state in which the remote station is located
/R# where # is the district of the remote station
/ followed by the ARRL Section of the remote station

llm.prose_fact: When a US-licensed amateur station is operated by remote control and the remote transmitter is located in the United States, no additional indicator is required in the station identification.
llm.answer_explanation: FCC rules treat a remotely controlled US transmitter as a normal US amateur station for identification purposes. As long as you properly transmit your FCC-issued call sign, no extra suffix or location indicator is needed on the ID when the remote transmitter is in the US.
llm.last_candidate: When a US-licensed amateur station is operated by remote control and the remote transmitter is located in the United States, no additional indicator is required in the station identification.

questions.question_text: Which of the following file formats is used for exchanging amateur radio log data?

llm.prose_fact: The ADIF file format is used for exchanging amateur radio log data.
llm.answer_explanation: ADIF (Amateur Data Interchange Format) is a standardized format designed specifically for sharing and importing/exporting amateur radio logbook information between different logging and award-tracking software. It allows consistent, structured transfer of contact records in the amateur radio context.
llm.last_candidate: The ADIF file format is used for exchanging amateur radio log data.

questions.question_text: From which of the following bands is amateur radio contesting generally excluded?

llm.prose_fact: Amateur radio contesting is generally excluded from the 30-meter band.
llm.answer_explanation: The 30-meter band is designated for narrow-band, non-contest operation to reduce interference and support shared use with other services. For this reason, contest activity is not allowed there under standard amateur radio operating practices and rules.
llm.last_candidate: Amateur radio contesting is generally excluded from the 30-meter band.

questions.question_text: Which of the following frequencies can be used for amateur radio mesh networks?
choices: HF frequencies where digital communications are permitted
Frequencies shared with various unlicensed wireless data services
The 60-meter band channel centered on 5373 kHz

llm.prose_fact: Amateur radio mesh networks can use frequencies that are shared with various unlicensed wireless data services.
llm.answer_explanation: These shared Part 97/Part 15 bands let amateurs legally operate mesh networking equipment that is often based on common unlicensed wireless hardware. This alignment allows hams to adapt off-the-shelf data equipment for amateur radio mesh network use within FCC rules.
llm.last_candidate: Amateur radio mesh networks can use frequencies that are shared with various unlicensed wireless data services.

questions.question_text: What is the function of a DX QSL Manager?
choices: Handle the receiving and sending of confirmations for a DX station
Run a net to allow many stations to contact a rare DX station
Communicate to a DXpedition about propagation, band openings, pileup conditions, etc.

llm.prose_fact: The function of a DX QSL Manager is to handle the receiving and sending of confirmations for a DX station.
llm.answer_explanation: A DX QSL manager centrally manages incoming and outgoing QSL confirmations on behalf of a DX station to ensure contacts are properly confirmed. This simplifies QSL processing for rare or busy DX stations and helps operators confirm contacts for awards and logging accuracy.
llm.last_candidate: The function of a DX QSL Manager is to handle the receiving and sending of confirmations for a DX station.

questions.question_text: During a VHF/UHF contest, in which band segment would you expect to find the highest level of SSB or CW activity?
choices: At the top of each band, usually in a segment reserved for contests
In the middle of each band, usually on the national calling frequency
In the weak signal segment of the band, with most of the activity near the calling frequency
In the middle of the band, usually 25 kHz above the national calling frequency

llm.prose_fact: During a VHF/UHF contest, you would expect to find the highest level of SSB or CW activity in the weak signal segment of the band, with most of the activity near the calling frequency.
llm.answer_explanation: VHF/UHF weak signal segments and their calling frequencies are the standard gathering points for SSB and CW operators, especially during contests. Concentrating activity near the calling frequency makes it easier for stations to find each other and make contacts efficiently under contest conditions.
llm.last_candidate: During a VHF/UHF contest, you would expect to find the highest level of SSB or CW activity in the weak signal segment of the band, with most of the activity near the calling frequency.

questions.question_text: What is the Cabrillo format?
choices: A standard for submission of electronic contest logs
A method of exchanging information during a contest QSO
The most common set of contest rules
A digital protocol specifically designed for rapid contest exchanges

llm.prose_fact: The Cabrillo format is a standard for submission of electronic contest logs.
llm.answer_explanation: In amateur radio contesting, Cabrillo defines a uniform text-file structure so that logging software and contest sponsors can reliably process and score electronic log submissions. Using this standard format ensures accurate and efficient handling of contest entries by log-checking systems.
llm.last_candidate: The Cabrillo format is a standard for submission of electronic contest logs.

questions.question_text: Which of the following contacts may be confirmed through the Logbook of The World (LoTW)?
choices: Special event contacts between stations in the US
Contacts between a US station and a non-US station
Contacts for Worked All States credit

llm.prose_fact: The Logbook of The World (LoTW) system can be used to confirm all types of amateur radio contacts that it supports, including domestic and DX QSOs, for award and confirmation purposes.
llm.answer_explanation: LoTW is the ARRL’s official online contact confirmation and awards system and is designed to confirm a wide range of amateur radio QSOs. In the exam context, this means that every contact type listed in the question can be confirmed through LoTW.
llm.last_candidate: The Logbook of The World (LoTW) system can be used to confirm all types of amateur radio contacts that it supports, including domestic and DX QSOs, for award and confirmation purposes.

questions.question_text: What type of equipment is commonly used to implement an amateur radio mesh network?
choices: A 2-meter VHF transceiver with a 1,200-baud modem
A computer running EchoLink to provide interface from the radio to the internet
A wireless router running custom firmware
A 440 MHz transceiver with a 9,600-baud modem

llm.prose_fact: An amateur radio mesh network is commonly implemented using a wireless router running custom firmware.
llm.answer_explanation: Custom firmware on a wireless router allows control of frequencies, power levels, and networking features suitable for amateur radio mesh networks. This enables flexible, IP-based communication links that comply with Amateur Extra operating practices.
llm.last_candidate: An amateur radio mesh network is commonly implemented using a wireless router running custom firmware.

questions.question_text: Why do DX stations often transmit and receive on different frequencies?
choices: Because the DX station may be transmitting on a frequency that is prohibited to some responding stations
To separate the calling stations from the DX station
To improve operating efficiency by reducing interference

llm.prose_fact: DX stations often transmit and receive on different frequencies in order to reduce interference, to shift the interfering signal from the station frequency, and to allow stations to call and listen on different frequencies.
llm.answer_explanation: Splitting transmit and receive frequencies (split operation) helps DX stations manage heavy calling, avoid interference on their transmit frequency, and maintain clearer communication. In the Amateur Extra exam context, all listed reasons together explain why DX stations commonly use split frequency operation.
llm.last_candidate: DX stations often transmit and receive on different frequencies in order to reduce interference, to shift the interfering signal from the station frequency, and to allow stations to call and listen on different frequencies.

questions.question_text: How should you generally identify your station when attempting to contact a DX station during a contest or in a pileup?
choices: Send your full call sign once or twice
Send only the last two letters of your call sign until you make contact
Send your full call sign and grid square
Send the call sign of the DX station three times, the words “this is,” then your call sign three times

llm.prose_fact: When attempting to contact a DX station during a contest or in a pileup, you should generally identify your station by sending your full call sign once or twice.
llm.answer_explanation: Sending your full call sign once or twice provides clear, complete identification without causing unnecessary congestion in a crowded operating situation. This practice improves efficiency and helps the DX station quickly log and respond to your call, which is especially important under contest and pileup conditions.
llm.last_candidate: When attempting to contact a DX station during a contest or in a pileup, you should generally identify your station by sending your full call sign once or twice.

questions.question_text: What indicates the delay between a control operator action and the corresponding change in the transmitted signal?

llm.prose_fact: Latency indicates the delay between a control operator action and the corresponding change in the transmitted signal.
llm.answer_explanation: In amateur radio systems, latency is the term used for the time lag between when an operator issues a control input and when the transmitted signal actually reflects that change. This delay is important in evaluating real-time responsiveness of remote or digital control links.
llm.last_candidate: Latency indicates the delay between a control operator action and the corresponding change in the transmitted signal.

questions.question_text: Which of the following digital modes is designed for meteor scatter communications?

llm.prose_fact: MSK144 is the digital mode specifically designed for meteor scatter communications.
llm.answer_explanation: MSK144 was developed to efficiently exchange brief data bursts reflected from ionized meteor trails. Its timing, encoding, and structure are optimized for the short, sporadic openings typical of meteor scatter on VHF bands.
llm.last_candidate: MSK144 is the digital mode specifically designed for meteor scatter communications.

questions.question_text: What information replaces signal-to-noise ratio when using the FT8 or FT4 modes in a VHF contest?

llm.prose_fact: When using the FT8 or FT4 modes in a VHF contest, grid square information replaces signal-to-noise ratio in the exchange.
llm.answer_explanation: In VHF contests using FT8 or FT4, the required contest exchange typically uses grid squares to indicate station location instead of reporting signal-to-noise ratio.
