This allows operators to determine distance and scoring based on Maidenhead grid locators, which is standard in VHF contest operation.
llm.last_candidate: When using the FT8 or FT4 modes in a VHF contest, grid square information replaces signal-to-noise ratio in the exchange.

questions.question_text: Which of the following digital modes is designed for EME communications?

llm.prose_fact: Q65 is a digital mode specifically designed for Earth-Moon-Earth (EME) communications.
llm.answer_explanation: Q65 was developed to optimize weak-signal digital communication via the Moon, matching the special timing, Doppler shift, and signal-to-noise challenges of EME paths. For the Amateur Extra exam, remember that Q65 is the dedicated modern digital mode for EME work.
llm.last_candidate: Q65 is a digital mode specifically designed for Earth-Moon-Earth (EME) communications.

questions.question_text: What technology is used for real-time tracking of balloons carrying amateur radio transmitters?

llm.prose_fact: Real-time tracking of balloons carrying amateur radio transmitters is commonly accomplished using APRS (Automatic Packet Reporting System).
llm.answer_explanation: APRS is a packet-based system that sends position and status data in real time over amateur radio frequencies. This makes it well-suited for tracking moving stations such as high-altitude balloons during Amateur Extra operations.
llm.last_candidate: Real-time tracking of balloons carrying amateur radio transmitters is commonly accomplished using APRS (Automatic Packet Reporting System).

questions.question_text: What is the characteristic of the JT65 mode?
choices: Decodes signals with a very low signal-to-noise ratio
Permits fast-scan TV transmissions over narrow bandwidth

llm.prose_fact: The JT65 digital mode is characterized by its ability to decode signals with a very low signal-to-noise ratio.
llm.answer_explanation: JT65 is specifically designed for weak-signal communication, allowing reliable decoding even when signals are far below the noise floor. This makes it especially useful for long-distance and marginal propagation paths in amateur radio.
llm.last_candidate: The JT65 digital mode is characterized by its ability to decode signals with a very low signal-to-noise ratio.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a method for establishing EME contacts?
choices: Judging optimum transmission times by monitoring beacons reflected from the moon
High-speed CW identification to avoid fading

llm.prose_fact: A method for establishing EME contacts is to use time-synchronous transmissions that alternate between the participating stations.
llm.answer_explanation: On EME paths, precise timing and alternating transmit/receive periods let very weak signals be decoded despite long delays and fading. Using synchronized time intervals ensures both stations are listening when the other is transmitting, improving the chance of completing a contact.
llm.last_candidate: A method for establishing EME contacts is to use time-synchronous transmissions that alternate between the participating stations.

questions.question_text: What digital protocol is used by APRS?

llm.prose_fact: The digital protocol used by APRS is AX.25.
llm.answer_explanation: APRS (Automatic Packet Reporting System) is built on the AX.25 packet radio protocol, which defines how digital data is formatted and transmitted over amateur radio. Knowing this association helps operators understand how APRS packets are structured and handled on the air.
llm.last_candidate: The digital protocol used by APRS is AX.25.

questions.question_text: What type of packet frame is used to transmit APRS beacon data?

llm.prose_fact: APRS beacon data is transmitted using an Unnumbered Information packet frame.
llm.answer_explanation: In the APRS system, beacon messages are sent as Unnumbered Information frames because they are simple, unacknowledged information packets. This frame type is well suited for periodic position and status reports that do not require connection-oriented delivery.
llm.last_candidate: APRS beacon data is transmitted using an Unnumbered Information packet frame.

questions.question_text: What type of modulation is used by JT65?

llm.prose_fact: JT65 uses multitone audio frequency-shift keying (AFSK) modulation.
llm.answer_explanation: JT65 signals are generated as multiple distinct audio tones that are frequency-shift keyed and then transmitted using the radio’s SSB mode, which makes the modulation type multitone AFSK in the Amateur Extra exam context. This multitone AFSK scheme enables very weak-signal communication with precise, time-synchronized digital encoding and decoding.
llm.last_candidate: JT65 uses multitone audio frequency-shift keying (AFSK) modulation.

questions.question_text: What does the packet path WIDE3-1 designate?
choices: Three stations are allowed on frequency, one transmitting at a time
Three subcarriers are permitted, subcarrier one is being used
Three digipeater hops are requested with one remaining
Three internet gateway stations may receive one transmission

llm.prose_fact: The packet path WIDE3-1 designates that three digipeater hops were originally requested and that one digipeater hop remains available to be used.
llm.answer_explanation: In APRS and packet routing, a path like WIDE3-1 shows how many digipeater hops were requested and how many are still unused. WIDE3-1 means the packet started with three requested hops and there is one remaining hop that can still be used by a digipeater.
llm.last_candidate: The packet path WIDE3-1 designates that three digipeater hops were originally requested and that one digipeater hop remains available to be used.

questions.question_text: How do APRS stations relay data?

llm.prose_fact: APRS stations relay data by packet digipeaters.
llm.answer_explanation: In the Amateur Packet Reporting System (APRS), stations use packet digipeaters to automatically receive, retransmit, and extend the range of position and message data over a wider area. This packet-based relaying method is a standard part of APRS network infrastructure on the amateur bands.
llm.last_candidate: APRS stations relay data by packet digipeaters.

questions.question_text: Which of the following types of modulation is used for data emissions below 30 MHz?
choices: DTMF tones modulating an FM signal

llm.prose_fact: FSK modulation is used for data emissions below 30 MHz.
llm.answer_explanation: Frequency Shift Keying (FSK) is a common and efficient digital modulation method used on HF bands for data communications. It is well suited to the propagation characteristics and bandwidth limitations of frequencies below 30 MHz in the amateur radio service.
llm.last_candidate: FSK modulation is used for data emissions below 30 MHz.

questions.question_text: Which of the following synchronizes WSJT-X digital mode transmit/receive timing?

llm.prose_fact: WSJT-X digital mode transmit and receive timing is synchronized by synchronization of computer clocks.
llm.answer_explanation: WSJT-X depends on very accurate time alignment between stations, so each station’s computer clock must be synchronized. This clock synchronization ensures the software starts and stops transmitting and receiving at precisely coordinated times for successful decoding.
llm.last_candidate: WSJT-X digital mode transmit and receive timing is synchronized by synchronization of computer clocks.

questions.question_text: To what does the "4" in FT4 refer?
choices: Multiples of 4 bits of user information

llm.prose_fact: In the context of the FT4 digital mode used on the amateur radio bands, the "4" refers to its use of four-tone continuous-phase frequency shift keying.
llm.answer_explanation: FT4 is a digital communication mode that encodes data by shifting among four discrete frequency tones in a continuous-phase FSK scheme, which is what the numeral "4" designates. This modulation approach is chosen to provide reliable, machine-decoded communication under typical HF propagation conditions for weak signals.
llm.last_candidate: In the context of the FT4 digital mode used on the amateur radio bands, the "4" refers to its use of four-tone continuous-phase frequency shift keying.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is characteristic of the FST4 mode?

llm.prose_fact: The FST4 mode is a digital communication mode designed for very weak signal work that uses fixed-length structured transmissions with selectable time periods and narrow bandwidth, and incorporates forward error correction, time synchronization, and low-rate data encoding to optimize performance on paths such as MF and LF propagation, EME, and other challenging propagation modes.
llm.answer_explanation: FST4 is engineered as a weak-signal digital mode that combines narrow bandwidth, selectable time periods, and strong error correction to reliably decode signals near the noise floor. These design characteristics make it well suited for difficult paths such as LF/MF, EME, and other low signal-to-noise conditions encountered in Amateur Extra-level operation.
llm.last_candidate: The FST4 mode is a digital communication mode designed for very weak signal work that uses fixed-length structured transmissions with selectable time periods and narrow bandwidth, and incorporates forward error correction, time synchronization, and low-rate data encoding to optimize performance on paths such as MF and LF propagation, EME, and other challenging propagation modes.

questions.question_text: Which of these digital modes does not support keyboard-to-keyboard operation?

llm.prose_fact: WSPR is a digital mode that does not support real-time keyboard-to-keyboard operation.
llm.answer_explanation: WSPR is specifically designed for low-power propagation reporting, not for interactive text communication. In the Amateur Extra exam context, it is identified as a beacon-style mode rather than a conversational keyboard-to-keyboard mode.
llm.last_candidate: WSPR is a digital mode that does not support real-time keyboard-to-keyboard operation.

questions.question_text: What is the length of an FT8 transmission cycle?
choices: It varies with the amount of data

llm.prose_fact: The length of an FT8 transmission cycle is 15 seconds.
llm.answer_explanation: FT8 is designed around a fixed 15-second cycle that includes both transmission and reception periods. This timing structure allows multiple synchronized contacts to occur efficiently within each 15-second window on the amateur bands.
llm.last_candidate: The length of an FT8 transmission cycle is 15 seconds.

questions.question_text: How does Q65 differ from JT65?

llm.prose_fact: Q65 differs from JT65 in that Q65 uses multiple receive cycles that are averaged together.
llm.answer_explanation: In weak-signal digital modes used on VHF, UHF, and higher bands, Q65 improves decoding sensitivity by averaging data over several receive cycles. This time-averaging enhances the ability to extract very weak signals compared to single-cycle decoding approaches.
llm.last_candidate: Q65 differs from JT65 in that Q65 uses multiple receive cycles that are averaged together.

questions.question_text: Which of the following HF digital modes can be used to transfer binary files?

llm.prose_fact: PACTOR is an HF digital mode that can be used to transfer binary files.
llm.answer_explanation: PACTOR supports reliable, error-corrected data transfer over HF, including the transmission of binary files within its protocol. This makes it suitable for applications like email and file transfer on the amateur HF bands under FCC rules.
llm.last_candidate: PACTOR is an HF digital mode that can be used to transfer binary files.

questions.question_text: Which of the following HF digital modes uses variable-length character coding?

llm.prose_fact: PSK31 is the HF digital mode that uses variable-length character coding.
llm.answer_explanation: PSK31 employs a varicode scheme where more common characters use shorter bit patterns and less common characters use longer ones. This variable-length coding improves efficiency and reduces bandwidth for HF keyboard-to-keyboard communication in the Amateur Extra exam context.
llm.last_candidate: PSK31 is the HF digital mode that uses variable-length character coding.

questions.question_text: Which of these digital modes has the narrowest bandwidth?

llm.prose_fact: FT8 is the digital mode among the listed choices that has the narrowest occupied bandwidth.
llm.answer_explanation: FT8 uses very short, structured transmissions with tightly controlled tones and timing, resulting in an extremely narrow signal bandwidth. This narrow bandwidth allows FT8 signals to be decoded reliably even under weak-signal and crowded band conditions, which is why it is considered the narrowest among the common digital modes on the exam.
llm.last_candidate: FT8 is the digital mode among the listed choices that has the narrowest occupied bandwidth.

questions.question_text: What is the difference between direct FSK and audio FSK?
choices: Direct FSK modulates the transmitter VFO
Direct FSK can transmit higher baud rates

llm.prose_fact: The difference between direct FSK and audio FSK is that direct FSK modulates the transmitter VFO.
llm.answer_explanation: In direct FSK, the digital signal shifts the frequency of the transmitter’s VFO directly to create the two keying tones. This method directly controls the RF carrier frequency instead of generating the shift through audio tones.
llm.last_candidate: The difference between direct FSK and audio FSK is that direct FSK modulates the transmitter VFO.

questions.question_text: How do ALE stations establish contact?
choices: ALE constantly scans a list of frequencies, activating the radio when the designated call sign is received
ALE radios monitor an internet site for the frequency they are being paged on
ALE radios send a constant tone code to establish a frequency for future use
ALE radios activate when they hear their signal echoed by back scatter

llm.prose_fact: Automatic Link Establishment (ALE) stations establish contact by constantly scanning a programmed list of frequencies and automatically activating the radio when the designated call sign is received.
llm.answer_explanation: ALE uses a prearranged frequency list and embedded call signs so stations can find each other and open a link without operator intervention. When a station detects its programmed call sign on one of the scanned frequencies, it automatically responds and establishes the connection, which is the behavior tested on the Amateur Extra exam.
llm.last_candidate: Automatic Link Establishment (ALE) stations establish contact by constantly scanning a programmed list of frequencies and automatically activating the radio when the designated call sign is received.

questions.question_text: Which of these digital modes has the highest data throughput under clear communication conditions?

llm.prose_fact: Under clear communication conditions, PACTOR IV provides the highest data throughput among common amateur radio digital modes.
llm.answer_explanation: PACTOR IV uses advanced modulation and coding techniques to maximize data rate when signals are strong and free of interference. In good HF conditions, it can transfer data significantly faster than other typical amateur digital modes, making it the highest-throughput choice on the exam.
llm.last_candidate: Under clear communication conditions, PACTOR IV provides the highest data throughput among common amateur radio digital modes.

questions.question_text: What is the approximate maximum separation measured along the surface of the Earth between two stations communicating by EME?
choices: 2,000 miles, if the moon is at perigee
2,000 miles, if the moon is at apogee
5,000 miles, if the moon is at perigee
12,000 miles, if the moon is “visible” by both stations

llm.prose_fact: The approximate maximum separation measured along the surface of the Earth between two stations communicating by EME is 12,000 miles, if the Moon is visible by both stations.
llm.answer_explanation: Earth-Moon-Earth (EME) communication requires that both stations have a line of sight to the Moon, which limits their maximum separation along Earth's surface to about 12,000 miles. This distance corresponds to the largest arc on the globe where the Moon can be simultaneously above the horizon for both locations.
llm.last_candidate: The approximate maximum separation measured along the surface of the Earth between two stations communicating by EME is 12,000 miles, if the Moon is visible by both stations.

questions.question_text: What characterizes libration fading of an EME signal?
choices: A slow change in the pitch of the CW signal
A gradual loss of signal as the sun rises
The returning echo is several hertz lower in frequency than the transmitted signal

llm.prose_fact: Libration fading of an EME signal is characterized by a fluttery, irregular fading.
llm.answer_explanation: On Earth-Moon-Earth (EME) paths, small changes in the reflecting surface of the Moon and in the propagation path cause rapid, uneven variations in signal strength. This produces the distinctive fluttery, irregular fading known as libration fading on EME signals.
llm.last_candidate: Libration fading of an EME signal is characterized by a fluttery, irregular fading.

questions.question_text: When scheduling EME contacts, which of these conditions will generally result in the least path loss?
choices: When the Moon is at perigee
When the Moon is at apogee
When the MUF is above 30 MHz

llm.prose_fact: When scheduling Earth-Moon-Earth (EME) contacts, the least path loss generally occurs when the Moon is at perigee.
llm.answer_explanation: Path loss is reduced when the distance between Earth and the Moon is minimized, which happens at perigee. The shorter path reduces free-space loss, improving signal strength for EME communication.
llm.last_candidate: When scheduling Earth-Moon-Earth (EME) contacts, the least path loss generally occurs when the Moon is at perigee.

questions.question_text: In what direction does an electromagnetic wave travel?
choices: It depends on the phase angle of the magnetic field
It travels parallel to the electric and magnetic fields
It depends on the phase angle of the electric field
It travels at a right angle to the electric and magnetic fields

llm.prose_fact: An electromagnetic wave travels in a direction that is at a right angle (perpendicular) to both its electric field and its magnetic field.
llm.answer_explanation: In free space, the electric and magnetic fields of an electromagnetic wave are mutually perpendicular and the wave propagates in the third, perpendicular direction. This right-angle relationship among the electric field, magnetic field, and direction of travel is fundamental to understanding radio wave propagation for the Amateur Extra exam.
llm.last_candidate: An electromagnetic wave travels in a direction that is at a right angle (perpendicular) to both its electric field and its magnetic field.

questions.question_text: How are the component fields of an electromagnetic wave oriented?
choices: They are 90 degrees out of phase

llm.prose_fact: The electric and magnetic fields that make up an electromagnetic wave are oriented at right angles to each other and to the direction of wave propagation.
llm.answer_explanation: In radio waves, the electric and magnetic components are mutually perpendicular, forming a transverse wave. This right-angle relationship is fundamental to how electromagnetic waves propagate in free space and is essential to understanding antenna polarization and radiation patterns.
llm.last_candidate: The electric and magnetic fields that make up an electromagnetic wave are oriented at right angles to each other and to the direction of wave propagation.

questions.question_text: What should be done to continue a long-distance contact when the MUF for that path decreases due to darkness?
choices: Switch to a higher frequency HF band
Switch to a lower frequency HF band
Change to an antenna with a higher takeoff angle
Change to an antenna with greater beam width

llm.prose_fact: To continue a long-distance contact when the MUF for that path decreases due to darkness, an amateur operator should switch to a lower frequency HF band.
llm.answer_explanation: As darkness lowers the Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF), higher HF bands may no longer support reliable ionospheric propagation on that path. Moving to a lower frequency HF band keeps the signal below the new MUF, allowing the contact to continue.
llm.last_candidate: To continue a long-distance contact when the MUF for that path decreases due to darkness, an amateur operator should switch to a lower frequency HF band.

questions.question_text: Atmospheric ducts capable of propagating microwave signals often form over what geographic feature?

llm.prose_fact: Atmospheric ducts capable of propagating microwave signals often form over large bodies of water.
llm.answer_explanation: Temperature inversions and stable air layers that create atmospheric ducts are commonly found over large bodies of water. These ducts can trap and guide microwave signals, allowing extended propagation ranges useful to amateur radio operators on microwave bands.
llm.last_candidate: Atmospheric ducts capable of propagating microwave signals often form over large bodies of water.

questions.question_text: When a meteor strikes the Earth’s atmosphere, a linear ionized region is formed at what region of the ionosphere?

llm.prose_fact: When a meteor strikes the Earth’s atmosphere, a linear ionized region is formed in the E region of the ionosphere.
llm.answer_explanation: Meteor trails typically ionize the atmosphere at altitudes corresponding to the E region of the ionosphere. This temporary ionized path can reflect VHF radio signals, enabling brief long-distance communication useful in amateur radio meteor-scatter modes.
llm.last_candidate: When a meteor strikes the Earth’s atmosphere, a linear ionized region is formed in the E region of the ionosphere.

questions.question_text: Which of the following frequency ranges is most suited for meteor-scatter communications?

llm.prose_fact: The frequency range most suited for meteor-scatter communications is 28 MHz to 148 MHz.
llm.answer_explanation: Meteor trails in the ionosphere efficiently reflect radio signals in the VHF and upper HF range, making 28 MHz to 148 MHz particularly effective for meteor-scatter contacts. This range balances good reflection efficiency with manageable path loss and antenna practicality for amateur radio use.
llm.last_candidate: The frequency range most suited for meteor-scatter communications is 28 MHz to 148 MHz.

questions.question_text: What determines the speed of electromagnetic waves through a medium?

llm.prose_fact: The speed of electromagnetic waves through a medium is determined by the medium’s index of refraction.
llm.answer_explanation: In radio and wave propagation theory, the index of refraction describes how much a medium slows electromagnetic waves compared to their speed in a vacuum. A higher index of refraction means a lower wave speed in that medium, which directly affects propagation behavior for amateur radio signals.
llm.last_candidate: The speed of electromagnetic waves through a medium is determined by the medium’s index of refraction.

questions.question_text: What is a typical range for tropospheric duct propagation of microwave signals?

llm.prose_fact: A typical range for tropospheric duct propagation of microwave signals is 100 miles to 300 miles.
llm.answer_explanation: Tropospheric ducting traps microwave signals in temperature inversion layers, allowing them to travel well beyond normal line-of-sight distances. Under typical ducting conditions, this extended range is about 100 miles to 300 miles for amateur radio microwave communication.
llm.last_candidate: A typical range for tropospheric duct propagation of microwave signals is 100 miles to 300 miles.

questions.question_text: What is most likely to result in auroral propagation?
choices: Extreme low-pressure areas in polar regions

llm.prose_fact: Auroral propagation is most likely to result from severe geomagnetic storms.
llm.answer_explanation: Severe geomagnetic storms disturb the Earth's magnetosphere and upper atmosphere, creating auroral ionization that can refract and scatter radio waves. This enhanced ionization along auroral zones enables unique VHF and sometimes HF propagation paths useful to amateur radio operators.
llm.last_candidate: Auroral propagation is most likely to result from severe geomagnetic storms.

questions.question_text: Which of these emission modes is best for auroral propagation?

llm.prose_fact: CW is the best emission mode for auroral propagation.
llm.answer_explanation: Auroral propagation distorts and scatters signals, and CW’s narrow bandwidth and simple on-off keying make it more likely to be intelligible under these rapid fading and flutter conditions. For this reason, CW is preferred for making reliable contacts via aurora in the Amateur Extra exam context.
llm.last_candidate: CW is the best emission mode for auroral propagation.

questions.question_text: What are circularly polarized electromagnetic waves?
choices: Waves with an electric field bent into a circular shape
Waves with rotating electric and magnetic fields
Waves produced by a loop antenna

llm.prose_fact: Circularly polarized electromagnetic waves are waves with rotating electric and magnetic fields.
llm.answer_explanation: In circular polarization, the electric and magnetic field vectors rotate in a circular manner as the wave propagates. This rotation defines the polarization type and is important for matching antenna polarization in advanced amateur radio communication links.
llm.last_candidate: Circularly polarized electromagnetic waves are waves with rotating electric and magnetic fields.

questions.question_text: Where is transequatorial propagation (TEP) most likely to occur?
choices: Between points separated by 2,000 miles to 3,000 miles over a path perpendicular to the geomagnetic equator
Between points located 1,500 miles to 2,000 miles apart on the geomagnetic equator
Between points located at each other’s antipode
Through the region where the terminator crosses the geographic equator

llm.prose_fact: Transequatorial propagation (TEP) is most likely to occur between points separated by 2,000 miles to 3,000 miles over a path that is perpendicular to the geomagnetic equator.
llm.answer_explanation: TEP is a VHF propagation mode guided by ionospheric structures aligned with Earth’s magnetic field, so paths that are roughly perpendicular to the geomagnetic equator and about 2,000 miles to 3,000 miles long best support it. This distance and orientation match the geometry of the ionization patterns that enable TEP on the Amateur Extra exam bands.
llm.last_candidate: Transequatorial propagation (TEP) is most likely to occur between points separated by 2,000 miles to 3,000 miles over a path that is perpendicular to the geomagnetic equator.

questions.question_text: What is the approximate maximum range for signals using transequatorial propagation?

llm.prose_fact: The approximate maximum range for signals using transequatorial propagation is 5,000 miles.
llm.answer_explanation: Transequatorial propagation supports long-distance HF communication by allowing signals to refract through ionospheric regions near the geomagnetic equator. Under typical conditions, this mode extends reliable communication paths up to about 5,000 miles.
llm.last_candidate: The approximate maximum range for signals using transequatorial propagation is 5,000 miles.

questions.question_text: At what time of day is transequatorial propagation most likely to occur?

llm.prose_fact: Transequatorial propagation is most likely to occur during the afternoon or early evening.
llm.answer_explanation: Afternoon and early evening are when the ionospheric conditions near the geomagnetic equator most often support transequatorial propagation. During this period, increased ionization enhances signal paths that cross the geomagnetic equator on VHF and higher HF frequencies, making contacts more likely.
llm.last_candidate: Transequatorial propagation is most likely to occur during the afternoon or early evening.

questions.question_text: What are “extraordinary” and “ordinary” waves?
choices: Extraordinary waves exhibit rare long-skip propagation, compared to ordinary waves, which travel shorter distances
Independently propagating, elliptically polarized waves created in the ionosphere

llm.prose_fact: In ionospheric propagation, “extraordinary” and “ordinary” waves are independently propagating, elliptically polarized waves created in the ionosphere.
llm.answer_explanation: The ionosphere splits a radio signal into two separate, elliptically polarized modes that travel independently, known as the ordinary and extraordinary waves. Recognizing these distinct ionospheric modes is important for understanding HF propagation behavior on the Amateur Extra exam.
llm.last_candidate: In ionospheric propagation, “extraordinary” and “ordinary” waves are independently propagating, elliptically polarized waves created in the ionosphere.

questions.question_text: Which of the following paths is most likely to support long-distance propagation on 160 meters?

llm.prose_fact: On 160 meters, a path that is entirely in darkness is most likely to support long-distance radio wave propagation.
llm.answer_explanation: On 160 meters, long-distance communication is favored when both the transmitting and receiving locations, as well as the path between them, are in darkness because ionospheric absorption is lower at night. This allows the low-frequency signals to reflect more efficiently from the ionosphere over greater distances, which is why an all-dark path is preferred for long-haul contacts on this band.
llm.last_candidate: On 160 meters, a path that is entirely in darkness is most likely to support long-distance radio wave propagation.

questions.question_text: On which of the following amateur bands is long-path propagation most frequent?

llm.prose_fact: Long-path propagation is most frequent on the 40-meter and 20-meter amateur bands.
llm.answer_explanation: The 40-meter and 20-meter bands most often have ionospheric conditions that support signals traveling the long way around the Earth. These bands commonly exhibit stable, long-distance paths that make long-path propagation more likely and more frequent for Amateur Extra operators to observe and use.
llm.last_candidate: Long-path propagation is most frequent on the 40-meter and 20-meter amateur bands.

questions.question_text: What effect does lowering a signal’s transmitted elevation angle have on ionospheric HF skip propagation?
choices: The distance covered by each hop increases

llm.prose_fact: Lowering a signal’s transmitted elevation angle increases the distance covered by each ionospheric HF skip hop.
llm.answer_explanation: A lower elevation angle sends the signal closer to the horizon, causing it to refract in the ionosphere and return to Earth farther away. This increases the single-hop skip distance on HF bands, which can extend overall communication range.
llm.last_candidate: Lowering a signal’s transmitted elevation angle increases the distance covered by each ionospheric HF skip hop.

questions.question_text: How does the maximum range of ground-wave propagation change when the signal frequency is increased?

llm.prose_fact: The maximum range of ground-wave propagation decreases as the signal frequency is increased.
llm.answer_explanation: Higher-frequency signals are absorbed more rapidly by the ground and lower atmosphere, which limits how far the ground wave can travel. Therefore, as frequency increases, the effective ground-wave coverage range becomes shorter for amateur radio communications.
llm.last_candidate: The maximum range of ground-wave propagation decreases as the signal frequency is increased.

questions.question_text: At what time of year is sporadic-E propagation most likely to occur?
choices: Around the solstices, especially the summer solstice
Around the solstices, especially the winter solstice
Around the equinoxes, especially the spring equinox
Around the equinoxes, especially the fall equinox

llm.prose_fact: Sporadic-E propagation is most likely to occur around the solstices, especially the summer solstice.
llm.answer_explanation: Sporadic-E propagation peaks near the summer and winter solstices due to seasonal variations in the ionosphere. For HF and VHF operators, this makes the summer solstice period particularly favorable for long-distance Es contacts.
llm.last_candidate: Sporadic-E propagation is most likely to occur around the solstices, especially the summer solstice.

questions.question_text: What is the effect of chordal-hop propagation?
choices: The signal experiences less loss compared to multi-hop propagation, which uses Earth as a reflector
The MUF for chordal-hop propagation is much lower than for normal skip propagation
Atmospheric noise is reduced in the direction of chordal-hop propagation
Signals travel faster along ionospheric chords

llm.prose_fact: Chordal-hop propagation causes a signal to experience less loss compared to multi-hop propagation that uses the Earth as a reflector.
llm.answer_explanation: In chordal-hop propagation, the signal travels between ionospheric layers without repeatedly reflecting from the Earth, which reduces absorption and scattering losses. This lower loss can result in stronger received signals over long-distance paths compared to conventional multi-hop paths that bounce off the Earth’s surface.
llm.last_candidate: Chordal-hop propagation causes a signal to experience less loss compared to multi-hop propagation that uses the Earth as a reflector.

questions.question_text: At what time of day is sporadic-E propagation most likely to occur?

llm.prose_fact: Sporadic-E propagation is most likely to occur between sunrise and sunset.
llm.answer_explanation: Sporadic-E propagation commonly peaks during daytime heating of the ionosphere, making the period between sunrise and sunset the most favorable time. This behavior is important for predicting VHF propagation conditions on the Amateur Extra exam.
llm.last_candidate: Sporadic-E propagation is most likely to occur between sunrise and sunset.

questions.question_text: What is chordal-hop propagation?
choices: Propagation away from the great circle bearing between stations
Successive ionospheric refractions without an intermediate reflection from the ground
Signals reflected back toward the transmitting station

llm.prose_fact: Chordal-hop propagation is successive ionospheric refractions without an intermediate reflection from the ground.
llm.answer_explanation: In chordal-hop propagation, the radio signal refracts multiple times between ionospheric layers while staying above the Earth’s surface, so it never reflects off the ground between hops. This can reduce signal loss compared to paths that include intermediate ground reflections, which is relevant to HF long-distance communication on the Amateur Extra exam.
llm.last_candidate: Chordal-hop propagation is successive ionospheric refractions without an intermediate reflection from the ground.

questions.question_text: What type of polarization is supported by ground-wave propagation?

llm.prose_fact: Ground-wave propagation supports vertical polarization.
llm.answer_explanation: Ground waves follow the Earth's surface most effectively when the transmitting and receiving antennas are vertically polarized. This is why vertically polarized antennas are commonly used for low-frequency and medium-frequency ground-wave communication in amateur radio.
llm.last_candidate: Ground-wave propagation supports vertical polarization.

questions.question_text: What is the cause of short-term radio blackouts?

llm.prose_fact: Short-term radio blackouts are caused by solar flares.
llm.answer_explanation: Solar flares can suddenly increase X-ray and extreme ultraviolet radiation from the Sun, which rapidly ionizes the Earth’s upper atmosphere and disrupts high-frequency radio propagation. In the Amateur Extra exam context, short-term HF communication outages are attributed to these flare events.
llm.last_candidate: Short-term radio blackouts are caused by solar flares.

questions.question_text: What is indicated by a rising A-index or K-index?
choices: Increasing disturbance of the geomagnetic field
Decreasing disturbance of the geomagnetic field
Higher levels of solar UV radiation
An increase in the critical frequency

llm.prose_fact: A rising A-index or K-index indicates increasing disturbance of the geomagnetic field.
llm.answer_explanation: The A-index and K-index are standard measures of geomagnetic activity, so higher values mean the Earth's magnetic field is becoming more disturbed. Such disturbances can adversely affect HF propagation and increase radio communication disruptions, which is why tracking these indices is important to amateur operators.
llm.last_candidate: A rising A-index or K-index indicates increasing disturbance of the geomagnetic field.

questions.question_text: Which of the following signal paths is most likely to experience high levels of absorption when the A-index or K-index is elevated?

llm.prose_fact: When the A-index or K-index is elevated, radio signals traveling through the auroral oval are most likely to experience high levels of absorption.
llm.answer_explanation: Elevated A and K indices indicate increased geomagnetic and auroral activity, which enhances ionospheric disturbances in the auroral oval. These disturbed regions cause strong signal absorption, especially affecting paths that traverse the auroral zone.
llm.last_candidate: When the A-index or K-index is elevated, radio signals traveling through the auroral oval are most likely to experience high levels of absorption.

questions.question_text: What does the value of Bz (B sub z) represent?
choices: North-south strength of the interplanetary magnetic field

llm.prose_fact: The value of Bz (B sub z) represents the north-south strength of the interplanetary magnetic field.
llm.answer_explanation: Bz is the component of the interplanetary magnetic field oriented in the north-south direction, which is important in radio propagation because it influences how the solar wind interacts with Earth's magnetosphere. This interaction can significantly affect HF and VHF propagation conditions relevant to amateur radio operation.
llm.last_candidate: The value of Bz (B sub z) represents the north-south strength of the interplanetary magnetic field.

questions.question_text: What orientation of Bz (B sub z) increases the likelihood that charged particles from the Sun will cause disturbed conditions?

llm.prose_fact: A southward orientation of Bz (B sub z) increases the likelihood that charged particles from the Sun will cause disturbed conditions.
llm.answer_explanation: When the interplanetary magnetic field’s Bz component points southward, it more easily couples with Earth’s northward-pointing magnetic field. This magnetic reconnection allows more charged solar particles to enter Earth’s magnetosphere, increasing geomagnetic disturbance and radio propagation effects.
llm.last_candidate: A southward orientation of Bz (B sub z) increases the likelihood that charged particles from the Sun will cause disturbed conditions.

questions.question_text: How does the VHF/UHF radio horizon compare to the geographic horizon?

llm.prose_fact: The VHF/UHF radio horizon is approximately 15 percent farther than the geographic horizon.
llm.answer_explanation: Because VHF/UHF signals can slightly follow the curvature of the Earth due to atmospheric refraction, the radio horizon extends about 15 percent beyond the visible (geographic) horizon. This extended range is important for estimating line-of-sight communication distances on these bands.
llm.last_candidate: The VHF/UHF radio horizon is approximately 15 percent farther than the geographic horizon.

questions.question_text: Which of the following indicates the greatest solar flare intensity?

llm.prose_fact: The greatest solar flare intensity is indicated by Class X.
llm.answer_explanation: Solar flares are categorized by intensity in ascending order of A, B, C, M, and X, with Class X representing the most powerful events. For radio amateurs, Class X flares are most associated with severe HF propagation disturbances and radio blackouts.
llm.last_candidate: The greatest solar flare intensity is indicated by Class X.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the space-weather term for an extreme geomagnetic storm?

llm.prose_fact: The space-weather term for an extreme geomagnetic storm is G5.
llm.answer_explanation: Space-weather agencies use the G-scale, from G1 through G5, to classify geomagnetic storm intensity, and G5 denotes the most severe, or extreme, storm level. Knowing this term helps amateur radio operators understand alerts that indicate potential major disruptions to HF propagation and other radio systems.
llm.last_candidate: The space-weather term for an extreme geomagnetic storm is G5.

questions.question_text: What type of data is reported by amateur radio propagation reporting networks?

llm.prose_fact: Amateur radio propagation reporting networks report data on digital-mode and CW signals.
llm.answer_explanation: These networks monitor and collect reception reports for digital-mode and CW transmissions to characterize current propagation conditions. By aggregating these reports, operators can see where and how well their signals are being received over time and distance.
llm.last_candidate: Amateur radio propagation reporting networks report data on digital-mode and CW signals.

questions.question_text: What does the 304A solar parameter measure?
choices: The ratio of X-ray flux to radio flux, correlated to sunspot number
UV emissions at 304 angstroms, correlated to the solar flux index
The solar wind velocity at an angle of 304 degrees from the solar equator, correlated to geomagnetic storms
The solar emission at 304 GHz, correlated to X-ray flare levels

llm.prose_fact: The 304A solar parameter measures ultraviolet (UV) emissions at 304 angstroms, which are correlated to the solar flux index.
llm.answer_explanation: The 304A parameter tracks UV output at a specific wavelength that relates closely to overall solar activity. Because of this correlation, it is useful to amateur radio operators as an indicator of propagation conditions similar to the solar flux index.
llm.last_candidate: The 304A solar parameter measures ultraviolet (UV) emissions at 304 angstroms, which are correlated to the solar flux index.

questions.question_text: What does VOACAP software model?

llm.prose_fact: VOACAP software models HF propagation.
llm.answer_explanation: VOACAP is a prediction tool that calculates how radio signals will propagate on HF bands under given conditions. It helps operators estimate coverage and signal reliability for HF communication paths, which is essential for Amateur Extra exam topics on propagation prediction tools.
llm.last_candidate: VOACAP software models HF propagation.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is indicated by a sudden rise in radio background noise across a large portion of the HF spectrum?
choices: A coronal mass ejection impact or a solar flare has occurred
Transequatorial propagation on 6 meters is likely
Long-path propagation on the higher HF bands is likely

llm.prose_fact: A sudden rise in radio background noise across a large portion of the HF spectrum indicates that a coronal mass ejection impact or a solar flare has occurred.
llm.answer_explanation: Large solar events such as coronal mass ejections and solar flares can rapidly increase radio noise levels over wide HF frequency ranges. Recognizing this effect helps operators understand that space weather is affecting HF propagation and communications.
llm.last_candidate: A sudden rise in radio background noise across a large portion of the HF spectrum indicates that a coronal mass ejection impact or a solar flare has occurred.

questions.question_text: Which of the following limits the highest frequency signal that can be accurately displayed on a digital oscilloscope?
choices: Sampling rate of the analog-to-digital converter

llm.prose_fact: On a digital oscilloscope, the highest frequency signal that can be accurately displayed is limited by the sampling rate of the analog-to-digital converter.
llm.answer_explanation: The sampling rate of the analog-to-digital converter determines how many times per second the input signal is measured, which sets the maximum frequency that can be reconstructed without distortion according to the Nyquist criterion. In the Amateur Extra exam context, recognizing that sampling rate governs the usable frequency range of a digital oscilloscope is essential for proper measurement and troubleshooting at higher RF frequencies.
llm.last_candidate: On a digital oscilloscope, the highest frequency signal that can be accurately displayed is limited by the sampling rate of the analog-to-digital converter.

questions.question_text: Which of the following parameters does a spectrum analyzer display on the vertical and horizontal axes?

llm.prose_fact: A spectrum analyzer displays signal amplitude on the vertical axis and frequency on the horizontal axis.
llm.answer_explanation: On a spectrum analyzer, the horizontal axis represents the range of frequencies being observed, while the vertical axis shows the amplitude (strength) of signals at those frequencies. This lets an amateur operator see how strong different signals are across the frequency spectrum.
llm.last_candidate: A spectrum analyzer displays signal amplitude on the vertical axis and frequency on the horizontal axis.

questions.question_text: Which of the following test instruments is used to display spurious signals and/or intermodulation distortion products generated by an SSB transmitter?

llm.prose_fact: A spectrum analyzer is used to display spurious signals and intermodulation distortion products generated by an SSB transmitter.
llm.answer_explanation: A spectrum analyzer shows signal amplitude versus frequency, allowing you to see unwanted spurious outputs and distortion products around the SSB signal. This makes it the appropriate instrument for evaluating the spectral purity of an SSB transmitter.
llm.last_candidate: A spectrum analyzer is used to display spurious signals and intermodulation distortion products generated by an SSB transmitter.

questions.question_text: How is compensation of an oscilloscope probe performed?
choices: A square wave is displayed, and the probe is adjusted until the horizontal portions of the displayed wave are as nearly flat as possible
A high frequency sine wave is displayed, and the probe is adjusted for maximum amplitude
A frequency standard is displayed, and the probe is adjusted until the deflection time is accurate
A DC voltage standard is displayed, and the probe is adjusted until the displayed voltage is accurate

llm.prose_fact: Compensation of an oscilloscope probe is performed by displaying a square wave and adjusting the probe until the horizontal portions of the displayed wave are as nearly flat as possible.
llm.answer_explanation: A properly compensated probe ensures that the oscilloscope’s input and the probe’s capacitance are matched so fast edges of a square wave are displayed accurately. Adjusting for flat, level tops on the square wave confirms the probe is correctly compensated for accurate waveform measurements in amateur radio work.
llm.last_candidate: Compensation of an oscilloscope probe is performed by displaying a square wave and adjusting the probe until the horizontal portions of the displayed wave are as nearly flat as possible.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of using a prescaler with a frequency counter?
choices: Amplify low-level signals for more accurate counting
Multiply a higher frequency signal so a low-frequency counter can display the operating frequency
Prevent oscillation in a low-frequency counter circuit
Reduce the signal frequency to within the counter's operating range

llm.prose_fact: The purpose of using a prescaler with a frequency counter is to reduce the signal frequency to within the counter's operating range.
llm.answer_explanation: Many frequency counters cannot directly measure very high RF frequencies, so a prescaler divides the input signal down to a lower frequency they can handle. This allows accurate measurement of high-frequency signals used in Amateur Extra class operations.
llm.last_candidate: The purpose of using a prescaler with a frequency counter is to reduce the signal frequency to within the counter's operating range.

questions.question_text: What is the effect of aliasing on a digital oscilloscope when displaying a waveform?
choices: A false, jittery low-frequency version of the waveform is displayed
The waveform DC offset will be inaccurate
Calibration of the vertical scale is no longer valid
Excessive blanking occurs, which prevents display of the waveform

llm.prose_fact: On a digital oscilloscope, aliasing causes a false, jittery low-frequency version of the waveform to be displayed.
llm.answer_explanation: When the sampling rate of a digital oscilloscope is too low relative to the signal frequency, higher-frequency components fold back into lower frequencies, creating a misleading low-frequency display. This effect is seen as a jittery, incorrect representation of the actual waveform, which is important to recognize in precise Amateur Extra-class measurements.
llm.last_candidate: On a digital oscilloscope, aliasing causes a false, jittery low-frequency version of the waveform to be displayed.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is an advantage of using an antenna analyzer compared to an SWR bridge?
choices: Antenna analyzers automatically tune your antenna for resonance
Antenna analyzers compute SWR and impedance automatically
Antenna analyzers display a time-varying representation of the modulation envelope

llm.prose_fact: An advantage of using an antenna analyzer compared to an SWR bridge is that antenna analyzers compute SWR and impedance automatically.
llm.answer_explanation: An antenna analyzer measures and then directly displays both SWR and complex impedance, simplifying antenna tuning. This automatic computation saves time and reduces calculation errors during station setup and adjustment for the Amateur Extra exam context.
llm.last_candidate: An advantage of using an antenna analyzer compared to an SWR bridge is that antenna analyzers compute SWR and impedance automatically.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is used to measure SWR?

llm.prose_fact: Standing wave ratio (SWR) can be measured with an antenna analyzer, an RF power meter with a reverse power function, or an SWR bridge.
llm.answer_explanation: These instruments are all designed to evaluate how efficiently RF power is transferred from a transmitter to a load by indicating the SWR on a transmission line. In the Amateur Extra exam context, recognizing that multiple common station instruments can measure SWR is essential for proper station setup and troubleshooting.
llm.last_candidate: Standing wave ratio (SWR) can be measured with an antenna analyzer, an RF power meter with a reverse power function, or an SWR bridge.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is good practice when using an oscilloscope probe?
choices: Minimize the length of the probe's ground connection
Never use a high-impedance probe to measure a low-impedance circuit
Never use a DC-coupled probe to measure an AC circuit

llm.prose_fact: When using an oscilloscope probe, it is good practice to minimize the length of the probe's ground connection.
llm.answer_explanation: A short ground lead reduces inductance and loop area, which minimizes signal distortion and noise pickup. This is especially important in RF and high-frequency measurements for accurate waveform observation on the oscilloscope.
llm.last_candidate: When using an oscilloscope probe, it is good practice to minimize the length of the probe's ground connection.

questions.question_text: Which trigger mode is most effective when using an oscilloscope to measure a linear power supply’s output ripple?

llm.prose_fact: The most effective oscilloscope trigger mode for measuring a linear power supply’s output ripple is the Line trigger mode.
llm.answer_explanation: Line trigger mode locks the oscilloscope’s trigger to the AC mains frequency that drives the linear power supply, stabilizing the display of ripple that is synchronized to that line frequency. This makes the ripple waveform easier to view and measure accurately on the screen.
llm.last_candidate: The most effective oscilloscope trigger mode for measuring a linear power supply’s output ripple is the Line trigger mode.

questions.question_text: Which of the following can be measured with an antenna analyzer?
choices: Resonant frequency of a tuned circuit

llm.prose_fact: An antenna analyzer can be used to measure SWR, antenna impedance, and the resonant frequency of an antenna system.
llm.answer_explanation: Modern antenna analyzers are designed to display several key parameters of an antenna system, including SWR, impedance, and resonance. Because it provides all of these related measurements, all of the listed functions are correctly performed by an antenna analyzer in Amateur Extra exam context.
llm.last_candidate: An antenna analyzer can be used to measure SWR, antenna impedance, and the resonant frequency of an antenna system.

questions.question_text: Which of the following factors most affects the accuracy of a frequency counter?

llm.prose_fact: The factor that most affects the accuracy of a frequency counter is the accuracy of its time base.
llm.answer_explanation: A frequency counter measures frequency by counting cycles against a precise time reference, so any error in the internal time base directly causes a proportional error in the measured frequency. For high-accuracy measurements in Amateur Extra work, a stable and accurate time base (often from a high-quality crystal or GPS reference) is essential.
llm.last_candidate: The factor that most affects the accuracy of a frequency counter is the accuracy of its time base.

questions.question_text: What is the significance of voltmeter sensitivity expressed in ohms per volt?
choices: The full scale reading of the voltmeter multiplied by its ohms per volt rating is the input impedance of the voltmeter
The reading in volts multiplied by the ohms per volt rating will determine the power drawn by the device under test
The reading in ohms divided by the ohms per volt rating will determine the voltage applied to the circuit
The full scale reading in amps divided by ohms per volt rating will determine the size of shunt needed

llm.prose_fact: The significance of voltmeter sensitivity expressed in ohms per volt is that the full scale reading of the voltmeter multiplied by its ohms per volt rating is the input impedance of the voltmeter.
llm.answer_explanation: Ohms per volt tells how much resistance the meter presents per volt of full-scale range, so multiplying this rating by the selected full-scale voltage gives the total input impedance. In radio work, knowing this impedance is important to avoid loading the circuit being measured.
llm.last_candidate: The significance of voltmeter sensitivity expressed in ohms per volt is that the full scale reading of the voltmeter multiplied by its ohms per volt rating is the input impedance of the voltmeter.

questions.question_text: Which S parameter is equivalent to forward gain?

llm.prose_fact: The S parameter equivalent to forward gain is S21.
llm.answer_explanation: In S-parameter notation, S21 represents the transmission from port 1 to port 2, which corresponds to the forward gain of a two-port RF network. This is the parameter used to describe how much of the input signal at the input port appears at the output port in the forward direction in amateur radio equipment and RF design.
llm.last_candidate: The S parameter equivalent to forward gain is S21.

questions.question_text: Which S parameter represents input port return loss or reflection coefficient (equivalent to VSWR)?

llm.prose_fact: The S parameter S11 represents the input port return loss or reflection coefficient, which is equivalent to VSWR.
llm.answer_explanation: S11 describes how much of a signal applied to the input port is reflected back, which directly relates to return loss, reflection coefficient, and VSWR. In RF design and measurement, S11 is used to evaluate how well the input of a device is impedance matched to the source or transmission line.
llm.last_candidate: The S parameter S11 represents the input port return loss or reflection coefficient, which is equivalent to VSWR.

questions.question_text: What three test loads are used to calibrate an RF vector network analyzer?
choices: Short circuit, open circuit, and 50 ohms
Short circuit, open circuit, and resonant circuit
50 ohms through 1/8 wavelength, 1/4 wavelength, and 1/2 wavelength of coaxial cable

llm.prose_fact: The three test loads used to calibrate an RF vector network analyzer are a short circuit, an open circuit, and a 50-ohm load.
llm.answer_explanation: These three standards (short, open, and 50-ohm load) provide known reference conditions that allow the vector network analyzer to accurately correct its measurements. They establish a calibration baseline for reflection and impedance measurements in typical 50-ohm amateur radio systems.
llm.last_candidate: The three test loads used to calibrate an RF vector network analyzer are a short circuit, an open circuit, and a 50-ohm load.

questions.question_text: How much power is being absorbed by the load when a directional power meter connected between a transmitter and a terminating load reads 100 watts forward power and 25 watts reflected power?

llm.prose_fact: When a directional power meter between a transmitter and a terminating load reads 100 watts forward power and 25 watts reflected power, the load is absorbing 75 watts of power.
llm.answer_explanation: The power actually delivered to the load is the forward power minus the reflected power. Subtracting 25 watts from 100 watts gives 75 watts absorbed by the load in this transmission line setup.
llm.last_candidate: When a directional power meter between a transmitter and a terminating load reads 100 watts forward power and 25 watts reflected power, the load is absorbing 75 watts of power.

questions.question_text: What do the subscripts of S parameters represent?
choices: The port or ports at which measurements are made

llm.prose_fact: The subscripts of S parameters represent the port or ports at which measurements are made.
llm.answer_explanation: In RF network analysis, S-parameters describe how signals behave at different ports of a device, and the subscripts identify which input and output ports are involved in the measurement. This is important in amateur radio when analyzing multi-port components such as amplifiers, filters, and duplexers using network analyzers.
llm.last_candidate: The subscripts of S parameters represent the port or ports at which measurements are made.

questions.question_text: Which of the following can be used to determine the Q of a series-tuned circuit?
choices: The ratio of inductive reactance to capacitive reactance
The bandwidth of the circuit's frequency response
The resonant frequency of the circuit

llm.prose_fact: The Q of a series-tuned circuit can be determined from the bandwidth of the circuit's frequency response.
llm.answer_explanation: For a resonant circuit, Q is defined in terms of its center frequency and the bandwidth between the half-power points of its frequency response. Measuring that bandwidth allows calculation of Q for exam and design purposes.
llm.last_candidate: The Q of a series-tuned circuit can be determined from the bandwidth of the circuit's frequency response.

questions.question_text: Which of the following can be measured by a two-port vector network analyzer?

llm.prose_fact: A two-port vector network analyzer can be used to measure the frequency response of a filter.
llm.answer_explanation: A two-port vector network analyzer measures how signals of different frequencies are transmitted and reflected through a device, which directly characterizes a filter’s frequency response. This allows amateurs to evaluate how well a filter passes desired frequencies and attenuates unwanted ones for proper station performance.
llm.last_candidate: A two-port vector network analyzer can be used to measure the frequency response of a filter.

questions.question_text: Which of the following methods measures intermodulation distortion in an SSB transmitter?
choices: Modulate the transmitter using two RF signals having non-harmonically related frequencies and observe the RF output with a spectrum analyzer
Modulate the transmitter using two AF signals having non-harmonically related frequencies and observe the RF output with a spectrum analyzer
Modulate the transmitter using two AF signals having harmonically related frequencies and observe the RF output with a peak reading wattmeter
Modulate the transmitter using two RF signals having harmonically related frequencies and observe the RF output with a logic analyzer

llm.prose_fact: Intermodulation distortion in an SSB transmitter is measured by modulating the transmitter with two AF signals having non-harmonically related frequencies and observing the RF output with a spectrum analyzer.
llm.answer_explanation: Using two non-harmonically related audio tones causes intermodulation products to appear at predictable spacings in the RF output spectrum. A spectrum analyzer makes these products visible so their levels can be measured relative to the desired signals for SSB transmitter performance evaluation.
llm.last_candidate: Intermodulation distortion in an SSB transmitter is measured by modulating the transmitter with two AF signals having non-harmonically related frequencies and observing the RF output with a spectrum analyzer.

questions.question_text: Which of the following can be measured with a vector network analyzer?

llm.prose_fact: A vector network analyzer can be used to measure input impedance, transmission loss, reflection coefficient, and return loss of a device or network.
llm.answer_explanation: A vector network analyzer characterizes RF components by measuring how signals are transmitted and reflected, including both magnitude and phase. This allows accurate determination of impedance and loss-related parameters needed for Amateur Extra-level design and troubleshooting.
llm.last_candidate: A vector network analyzer can be used to measure input impedance, transmission loss, reflection coefficient, and return loss of a device or network.

questions.question_text: What is an effect of excessive phase noise in an SDR receiver’s master clock oscillator?
choices: It limits the receiver’s ability to receive strong signals
It can affect the receiver’s frequency calibration
It decreases the receiver’s third-order intercept point
It can combine with strong signals on nearby frequencies to generate interference

llm.prose_fact: Excessive phase noise in an SDR receiver’s master clock oscillator can combine with strong signals on nearby frequencies to generate interference.
llm.answer_explanation: In an SDR receiver, excessive phase noise spreads the energy of nearby strong signals, causing them to mix and produce interference products. This degrades receiver performance by raising the noise floor and masking weaker signals on or near the desired frequency.
llm.last_candidate: Excessive phase noise in an SDR receiver’s master clock oscillator can combine with strong signals on nearby frequencies to generate interference.

questions.question_text: Which of the following receiver circuits can be effective in eliminating interference from strong out-of-band signals?

llm.prose_fact: A receiver front-end filter or preselector can be effective in eliminating interference from strong out-of-band signals by attenuating unwanted frequencies before they reach later stages of the receiver.
llm.answer_explanation: Strong signals outside the desired band can overload the receiver front end, causing distortion and intermodulation. A front-end filter or preselector limits the receiver input to the desired frequency range, reducing the impact of strong out-of-band signals.
llm.last_candidate: A receiver front-end filter or preselector can be effective in eliminating interference from strong out-of-band signals by attenuating unwanted frequencies before they reach later stages of the receiver.

questions.question_text: What is the term for the suppression in an FM receiver of one signal by another stronger signal on the same frequency?

llm.prose_fact: The suppression in an FM receiver of one signal by another stronger signal on the same frequency is called the capture effect.
llm.answer_explanation: FM receivers tend to lock onto and demodulate the stronger of two signals on the same frequency, effectively suppressing the weaker one. This phenomenon is known in radio communications as the capture effect and is a characteristic behavior of FM detection circuits.
llm.last_candidate: The suppression in an FM receiver of one signal by another stronger signal on the same frequency is called the capture effect.

questions.question_text: What is the noise figure of a receiver?
choices: The ratio of atmospheric noise to phase noise
The ratio of the noise bandwidth in hertz to the theoretical bandwidth of a resistive network
The ratio in dB of the noise generated in the receiver to atmospheric noise
The ratio in dB of the noise generated by the receiver to the theoretical minimum noise

llm.prose_fact: The noise figure of a receiver is the ratio in dB of the noise generated by the receiver to the theoretical minimum noise.
llm.answer_explanation: Noise figure quantifies how much additional noise a receiver adds compared to an ideal, noise-free system at the same temperature and bandwidth. In amateur radio, a lower noise figure means the receiver can detect weaker signals more effectively above the theoretical noise floor.
llm.last_candidate: The noise figure of a receiver is the ratio in dB of the noise generated by the receiver to the theoretical minimum noise.

questions.question_text: What does a receiver noise floor of -174 dBm represent?
choices: The receiver noise is 6 dB above the theoretical minimum
The theoretical noise in a 1 Hz bandwidth at the input of a perfect receiver at room temperature
The noise figure of a 1 Hz bandwidth receiver
The receiver noise is 3 dB above theoretical minimum

llm.prose_fact: A receiver noise floor of -174 dBm represents the theoretical noise in a 1 Hz bandwidth at the input of a perfect receiver at room temperature.
llm.answer_explanation: -174 dBm in 1 Hz bandwidth is the fundamental thermal noise limit set by physics for a receiver at room temperature. It provides a reference point for comparing real receiver noise performance in amateur radio systems.
llm.last_candidate: A receiver noise floor of -174 dBm represents the theoretical noise in a 1 Hz bandwidth at the input of a perfect receiver at room temperature.

questions.question_text: How much does increasing a receiver’s bandwidth from 50 Hz to 1,000 Hz increase the receiver’s noise floor?

llm.prose_fact: Increasing a receiver’s bandwidth from 50 Hz to 1,000 Hz raises the receiver’s noise floor by 13 dB.
llm.answer_explanation: Receiver noise power is proportional to bandwidth, so increasing the bandwidth from 50 Hz to 1,000 Hz significantly increases the integrated noise. This bandwidth ratio corresponds to a 13 dB increase in the noise floor, which reduces the system’s ability to copy weak signals.
llm.last_candidate: Increasing a receiver’s bandwidth from 50 Hz to 1,000 Hz raises the receiver’s noise floor by 13 dB.

questions.question_text: What does the MDS of a receiver represent?

llm.prose_fact: The MDS of a receiver represents the minimum discernible signal.
llm.answer_explanation: In amateur radio receiver specifications, minimum discernible signal (MDS) is the smallest signal level that can be detected above the receiver’s noise floor. It is a key sensitivity measure that indicates how weak a signal the receiver can reliably hear under standard conditions.
llm.last_candidate: The MDS of a receiver represents the minimum discernible signal.

questions.question_text: An SDR receiver is overloaded when input signals exceed what level?
choices: One-half of the maximum sample rate
One-half of the maximum sampling buffer size
The maximum count value of the analog-to-digital converter
The reference voltage of the analog-to-digital converter

llm.prose_fact: An SDR receiver is overloaded when input signals exceed the reference voltage of the analog-to-digital converter.
llm.answer_explanation: In an SDR, the analog-to-digital converter (ADC) sets the maximum signal level it can accurately digitize. When the input exceeds the ADC reference voltage, the signal clips and causes overload distortion in the received signal.
llm.last_candidate: An SDR receiver is overloaded when input signals exceed the reference voltage of the analog-to-digital converter.

questions.question_text: Which of the following choices is a good reason for selecting a high IF for a superheterodyne HF or VHF communications receiver?
choices: Easier for front-end circuitry to eliminate image responses

llm.prose_fact: A good reason for selecting a high intermediate frequency (IF) for a superheterodyne HF or VHF communications receiver is that it is easier for the front-end circuitry to eliminate image responses.
llm.answer_explanation: Using a higher IF places image frequencies farther away from the desired signal, making them easier to reject with practical RF filtering at the receiver front end. This improves receiver performance by reducing unwanted signals that could otherwise mix into the passband.
llm.last_candidate: A good reason for selecting a high intermediate frequency (IF) for a superheterodyne HF or VHF communications receiver is that it is easier for the front-end circuitry to eliminate image responses.

questions.question_text: What is an advantage of having a variety of receiver bandwidths from which to select?
choices: The noise figure of the RF amplifier can be adjusted to match the modulation type, thus increasing receiver sensitivity
Receiver power consumption can be reduced when wider bandwidth is not required
Receive bandwidth can be set to match the modulation bandwidth, maximizing signal-to-noise ratio and minimizing interference
Multiple frequencies can be received simultaneously if desired

llm.prose_fact: An advantage of having a variety of receiver bandwidths from which to select is that the receive bandwidth can be set to match the modulation bandwidth, maximizing signal-to-noise ratio and minimizing interference.
llm.answer_explanation: When the receiver bandwidth closely matches the signal’s modulation bandwidth, less noise and unwanted signals are admitted, improving intelligibility. This is important in amateur radio to optimize reception under varying band conditions and signal types.
llm.last_candidate: An advantage of having a variety of receiver bandwidths from which to select is that the receive bandwidth can be set to match the modulation bandwidth, maximizing signal-to-noise ratio and minimizing interference.

questions.question_text: Why does input attenuation reduce receiver overload on the lower frequency HF bands with little or no impact on signal-to-noise ratio?
choices: The attenuator has a low-pass filter to increase the strength of lower frequency signals
The attenuator has a noise filter to suppress interference
Signals are attenuated separately from the noise
Atmospheric noise is generally greater than internally generated noise even after attenuation

llm.prose_fact: Input attenuation reduces receiver overload on the lower frequency HF bands with little or no impact on signal-to-noise ratio because atmospheric noise is generally greater than internally generated noise even after attenuation.
llm.answer_explanation: On the lower HF bands, the dominant noise source is atmospheric noise, not the receiver’s own internal noise. Adding attenuation lowers both the signal and the strong incoming noise, but since atmospheric noise still remains stronger than the receiver noise, the signal-to-noise ratio is essentially unchanged while overload is reduced.
llm.last_candidate: Input attenuation reduces receiver overload on the lower frequency HF bands with little or no impact on signal-to-noise ratio because atmospheric noise is generally greater than internally generated noise even after attenuation.

questions.question_text: How does a narrow-band roofing filter affect receiver performance?
choices: It improves sensitivity by reducing front-end noise
It improves intelligibility by using low Q circuitry to reduce ringing
It improves blocking dynamic range by attenuating strong signals near the receive frequency

llm.prose_fact: A narrow-band roofing filter improves receiver blocking dynamic range by attenuating strong signals that are near the receive frequency.
llm.answer_explanation: By sharply limiting the bandwidth early in the receiver, a narrow-band roofing filter reduces the energy from strong nearby signals that could otherwise overload later stages. This improves the receiver’s ability to handle strong adjacent signals without degrading reception of the desired signal, which is described as better blocking dynamic range in the Amateur Extra exam context.
llm.last_candidate: A narrow-band roofing filter improves receiver blocking dynamic range by attenuating strong signals that are near the receive frequency.

questions.question_text: What is reciprocal mixing?
choices: Two out-of-band signals mixing to generate an in-band spurious signal
In-phase signals cancelling in a mixer resulting in loss of receiver sensitivity
Two digital signals combining from alternate time slots
Local oscillator phase noise mixing with adjacent strong signals to create interference to desired signals

llm.prose_fact: Reciprocal mixing is interference to desired signals caused by local oscillator phase noise mixing with adjacent strong signals.
llm.answer_explanation: In a receiver, phase noise from the local oscillator can mix with nearby strong signals and produce noise in the passband of the desired weak signal. This degrades the effective sensitivity and dynamic range of the receiver, which is described as reciprocal mixing in the Amateur Extra exam context.
llm.last_candidate: Reciprocal mixing is interference to desired signals caused by local oscillator phase noise mixing with adjacent strong signals.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of the receiver IF Shift control?
choices: To permit listening on a different frequency from the transmitting frequency
To reduce interference from stations transmitting on adjacent frequencies
To tune in stations slightly off frequency without changing the transmit frequency

llm.prose_fact: The purpose of the receiver IF Shift control is to reduce interference from stations transmitting on adjacent frequencies.
llm.answer_explanation: By shifting the intermediate frequency passband, the IF Shift control moves interfering signals from nearby frequencies outside the receiver’s most sensitive passband. This helps the operator better copy the desired signal when strong adjacent-channel signals are present.
llm.last_candidate: The purpose of the receiver IF Shift control is to reduce interference from stations transmitting on adjacent frequencies.

questions.question_text: What is meant by the blocking dynamic range of a receiver?
choices: The difference in dB between the noise floor and the level of an incoming signal that will cause 1 dB of gain compression
The minimum difference in dB between the levels of two FM signals that will cause one signal to block the other
The difference in dB between the noise floor and the third-order intercept point
The minimum difference in dB between two signals which produce third-order intermodulation products greater than the noise floor

llm.prose_fact: The blocking dynamic range of a receiver is the difference in dB between the noise floor and the level of an incoming signal that will cause 1 dB of gain compression.
llm.answer_explanation: Blocking dynamic range describes how strong a nearby signal can be before it starts to compress the receiver’s gain by 1 dB. In Amateur Extra exam context, this measures how well a receiver can handle strong signals without degrading the ability to copy weaker signals near the noise floor.
llm.last_candidate: The blocking dynamic range of a receiver is the difference in dB between the noise floor and the level of an incoming signal that will cause 1 dB of gain compression.

questions.question_text: Which of the following describes problems caused by poor dynamic range in a receiver?
choices: Spurious signals caused by cross modulation and desensitization from strong adjacent signals
Oscillator instability requiring frequent retuning and loss of ability to recover the opposite sideband
Poor weak signal reception caused by insufficient local oscillator injection
Oscillator instability and severe audio distortion of all but the strongest received signals

llm.prose_fact: Poor dynamic range in a receiver causes spurious signals due to cross modulation and desensitization from strong adjacent signals.
llm.answer_explanation: Dynamic range defines how well a receiver can handle both weak and strong signals at the same time. When dynamic range is poor, strong nearby signals overload the receiver front end, creating spurious signals and reducing sensitivity to weaker signals (desensitization).
llm.last_candidate: Poor dynamic range in a receiver causes spurious signals due to cross modulation and desensitization from strong adjacent signals.

questions.question_text: What creates intermodulation interference between two repeaters in close proximity?
choices: The output signals cause feedback in the final amplifier of one or both transmitters
The output signals mix in the final amplifier of one or both transmitters
The input frequencies are harmonically related
The output frequencies are harmonically related

llm.prose_fact: Intermodulation interference between two repeaters in close proximity is created when their output signals mix in the final amplifier of one or both transmitters.
llm.answer_explanation: When strong RF signals from nearby repeaters are present at a transmitter’s final amplifier, they can mix nonlinearly and generate new, unwanted intermodulation products. These products can fall on other frequencies and cause interference, which is why close-proximity repeaters must be carefully engineered to avoid this condition.
llm.last_candidate: Intermodulation interference between two repeaters in close proximity is created when their output signals mix in the final amplifier of one or both transmitters.

questions.question_text: What is the term for the reduction in receiver sensitivity caused by a strong signal near the received frequency?

llm.prose_fact: Desensitization is the reduction in receiver sensitivity caused by a strong signal near the received frequency.
